docs.title: Advanced Mechanics & Properties of Matter Lab — User Documentation
docs.text:
Welcome to the companion user guide for the Advanced Mechanics & Properties of Matter Lab, an interactive virtual laboratory covering rotational dynamics, the elasticity of solids, the viscosity and surface tension of fluids, and oscillations, damping and acoustic methods at postgraduate level — 18 interactive experiments in all, plus a dedicated safety module and six industrial/applied-mechanics workshops (including three new practical-application experiments — a ballistic pendulum, a rolling-race incline, and gyroscopic precession).

This guide explains the theoretical background of every experiment and workshop in the simulator, walks through the procedure for using each interactive panel, and provides ready-to-use observation tables, graph instructions, and calculation/result templates so that the simulator can be used exactly like a physical laboratory session.

The lab is designed to extend and complement course topics such as BHP301 (Electricity & Magnetism) and the broader mechanics and properties-of-matter units of the UG physics curriculum, while several experiments (coupled oscillators, damping and resonance, and the ultrasonic interferometer) are pitched at PG level for students continuing into advanced mechanics and acoustics.

docs.title: How to Use This Guide
docs.text: Each experiment page follows the same structure: Aim → Theoretical Background → Governing Formulas → Simulator Controls → Procedure → Observation Table → Graph → Calculation → Result → Precautions → Viva-Voce Questions. Each workshop page follows the analogous structure for an industrial process rather than a measurement experiment.

docs.title: Formula Quick Reference
docs.text: A single-page cheat-sheet of every governing equation used across the 18 experiments, grouped the same way as the main Contents. Use this page while doing calculations so you don't have to open each experiment page individually — click the linked experiment name if you need the full derivation or context.

docs.title: Rotational Dynamics
docs.text:
1. Bifilar Suspension

\[ T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I l}{Mgb^{2}}} \qquad I = \dfrac{M g b^{2} T^{2}}{4\pi^{2} l} \]

2. Torsional Pendulum

\[ C = \dfrac{\pi\eta r^{4}}{2l} \qquad I = \dfrac{2md^{2}T^{2}}{T'^{2}-T^{2}} \qquad \eta = \dfrac{8\pi l I}{r^{4}T^{2}} \]

3. Flywheel

\[ \omega = \dfrac{2\pi n_{1}}{t} \qquad I = \dfrac{mr^{2}(2gh-\omega^{2}r^{2})}{\omega^{2}}\times\dfrac{n_{2}}{n_{1}+n_{2}} \]

4. Compound (Bar) Pendulum

\[ T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{k^{2}+h^{2}}{gh}} \qquad h_{1}h_{2}=k^{2} \qquad g = \dfrac{4\pi^{2}L}{T^{2}} \]

docs.title: Elasticity of Solids
docs.text:
5. Non-Uniform (Cantilever) Bending

\[ Y = \dfrac{4Wl^{3}}{bd^{3}y} \]

6. Uniform Bending

\[ Y = \dfrac{3Mga l^{2}}{2bd^{3}e} \]

7. Searle's Apparatus

\[ Y = \dfrac{MgL}{\pi r^{2} l_{\text{ext}}} \]

8. Static Torsion

\[ \theta = \dfrac{2Cl}{\pi\eta r^{4}} \qquad \eta = \dfrac{2Cl}{\pi r^{4}\theta} \]

docs.title: Fluid Properties: Viscosity & Surface Tension
docs.text:
9. Poiseuille's Method

\[ \eta = \dfrac{\pi P r^{4} t}{8Vl}, \qquad P=\rho g h \]

10. Stokes' Method

\[ \eta = \dfrac{2}{9}\dfrac{r^{2}g(\rho-\sigma)}{v} \]

11. Capillary Rise

\[ T = \dfrac{h r \rho g}{2} \]

docs.title: Oscillations, Damping & Acoustic Methods (PG)
docs.text:
12. Coupled Pendulums

\[ f_{s}=\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}} \qquad f_{a}=\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}+\dfrac{2k}{m}} \qquad T_{\text{beat}}=\dfrac{1}{f_{a}-f_{s}} \]

13. Damped Oscillations

\[ A_{n}=A_{0}e^{-\gamma t} \qquad \delta = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_{n}}{A_{n+1}}\right)=\gamma T \qquad Q\approx\dfrac{\pi}{\delta} \]

14. Forced Oscillations & Resonance

\[ A(\omega)=\dfrac{F_{0}/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_{0}^{2}-\omega^{2})^{2}+(2\gamma\omega)^{2}}} \qquad Q=\dfrac{f_{0}}{\Delta f} \]

15. Ultrasonic Interferometer

\[ \lambda = 2\overline{\Delta d} \qquad v=f\lambda \qquad \beta=\dfrac{1}{\rho v^{2}} \qquad K=\rho v^{2} \]

docs.title: Practical Applications
docs.text:
16. Ballistic Pendulum

\[ v = \dfrac{M+m}{m}\sqrt{2gh} \qquad 1-\cos\theta = \dfrac{m^{2}v^{2}}{2gL(M+m)^{2}} \]

17. Rolling Race on an Incline

\[ a = \dfrac{g\sin\theta}{1+k} \qquad I = kmr^{2} \qquad t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2L}{a}} \]

18. Gyroscopic Precession

\[ L = I\omega = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^{2}\omega \qquad \tau = Mgd \qquad \Omega = \dfrac{\tau}{L} = \dfrac{2gd}{R^{2}\omega} \]

docs.title: Glossary of Key Terms
docs.text: Definitions of the recurring physical concepts used across the 18 experiments and 6 workshops in this guide, in alphabetical order. Each entry links back to the experiment(s) where the term is most central.

docs.title: A
docs.text:
Adiabatic compressibility (β) — The fractional decrease in volume per unit increase in pressure of a medium under conditions of no heat exchange; related to the velocity of sound by β = 1/(ρv²). See Ultrasonic Interferometer.

Angle of twist (θ) — The angle through which one end of a wire rotates relative to the other under an applied torque; proportional to the applied couple for an elastic wire. See Static Torsion.

docs.title: B
docs.text:
Ballistic pendulum — A device that determines a projectile's speed by catching it in a swinging pendulum block, combining momentum conservation (during the catch) with energy conservation (during the swing). See Ballistic Pendulum.

Beat period (T_beat) — The period over which oscillatory energy visibly transfers back and forth between two weakly coupled oscillators with slightly different normal-mode frequencies. See Coupled Pendulums.

Bulk modulus (K) — A measure of a substance's resistance to uniform compression, K = ρv² for a fluid, where v is the speed of sound in it. See Ultrasonic Interferometer.

docs.title: C
docs.text: Conjugate points — A pair of pivot points on either side of the centre of gravity of a compound pendulum that give the same period of oscillation; the basis of Kater's reversible pendulum. See Compound (Bar) Pendulum.

docs.title: D
docs.text: Damping ratio (ζ) — A dimensionless measure of how strongly oscillations in a system are damped, relative to the critical damping value that just prevents oscillation altogether. See Damped Oscillations, Automotive Shock-Absorber Damping Design.

docs.title: E
docs.text: Elastic limit — The maximum stress a material can withstand while still returning completely to its original shape once the load is removed; beyond it, permanent (plastic) deformation occurs. See Non-Uniform (Cantilever) Bending, Structural Beam Load Testing.

docs.title: F
docs.text: Frictional couple (τ_f) — The torque exerted by friction at a rotating axle's bearings, opposing its rotation and dissipating its kinetic energy over time. See Flywheel.

docs.title: L
docs.text: Logarithmic decrement (δ) — The natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes in a damped oscillation, δ = ln(A_n/A_{n+1}); a direct, dimensionless measure of the damping strength. See Damped Oscillations.

docs.title: M
docs.text:
Maximum permissible error — The upper bound on the uncertainty in a calculated result, obtained by propagating the least-count (instrument resolution) errors of the individually measured quantities through the governing formula.

Moment of inertia (I) — The rotational analogue of mass: a measure of a body's resistance to angular acceleration about a given axis, depending on both its mass and how that mass is distributed relative to the axis. See Bifilar Suspension, Torsional Pendulum, Flywheel.

docs.title: N
docs.text: Normal mode — A pattern of motion in a coupled oscillating system in which every part of the system oscillates at the same single frequency; any general motion of the system can be written as a superposition of its normal modes. See Coupled Pendulums.

docs.title: P
docs.text:
Poiseuille's law — The relation giving the volume flow rate of a liquid through a narrow tube under laminar flow, proportional to the fourth power of the tube's radius. See Poiseuille's Method.

Precession — The slow, steady sweeping motion of a spinning body's rotation axis around another axis, caused by an applied torque that is perpendicular to its spin angular momentum. See Gyroscopic Precession.

docs.title: Q
docs.text: Quality factor (Q) — A dimensionless measure of how lightly damped an oscillator is; equivalently, the sharpness of its resonance peak, Q = f₀/Δf, or approximately π/δ from the decay of a free oscillation. See Damped Oscillations, Forced Oscillations & Resonance.

docs.title: R
docs.text:
Radius of gyration (k) — The distance from an axis at which the entire mass of a body could be concentrated to give the same moment of inertia about that axis. See Compound (Bar) Pendulum.

Resonance — The dramatic increase in the steady-state amplitude of a driven oscillator when the driving frequency approaches its natural frequency. See Forced Oscillations & Resonance.

Reynolds number — A dimensionless quantity that predicts whether flow will be laminar (low value) or turbulent (high value); Stokes' law and Poiseuille's law are both valid only at sufficiently low Reynolds number. See Stokes' Method, Poiseuille's Method.

Rigidity (shear) modulus (η) — The ratio of shear stress to shear strain for an elastic solid, characterising its resistance to twisting or shape-changing deformation at constant volume. See Torsional Pendulum, Static Torsion.

Rolling without slipping — Rolling motion in which the contact point of a body has zero instantaneous velocity relative to the surface, so its linear speed and angular speed are locked together by v = rω. See Rolling Race on an Incline.

docs.title: S
docs.text:
Stokes' law — The formula for the viscous drag force on a small sphere moving slowly through a fluid, proportional to the sphere's radius, its speed, and the fluid's viscosity. See Stokes' Method.

Surface tension (T) — The tendency of a liquid surface to behave like a stretched elastic membrane, arising from unbalanced intermolecular attraction at the surface. See Capillary Rise Method.

docs.title: T
docs.text:
Terminal velocity — The constant speed reached by an object falling through a fluid once the net force on it (weight, buoyancy and drag combined) becomes zero. See Stokes' Method.

Transmissibility — The ratio of the force (or motion) transmitted through a vibration isolator to the force (or motion) generated by the source; the central design quantity in vibration isolation. See Vibration Isolation & Machine Mounting.

docs.title: V
docs.text: Viscosity (η) — A fluid's internal resistance to relative motion between its layers (internal friction), governing how readily it flows under an applied pressure or shear. See Poiseuille's Method, Stokes' Method.

docs.title: Y
docs.text:
Young's modulus (Y) — The ratio of longitudinal (tensile or compressive) stress to strain for an elastic solid, characterising its stiffness along the direction of an applied load. See Non-Uniform Bending, Uniform Bending, Searle's Apparatus.

docs.title: Mechanical Lab Safety, Precautions & Potential Harm
docs.text:
Essential Reading Before Any Real Mechanics Experiment

Every experiment in this lab that involves a real falling mass, spinning flywheel, stretched wire, swinging pendulum, or pressurized/rotating workshop rig carries real mechanical hazards. Read this section before touching any physical apparatus.

Warning

Never place your fingers, hands, or face in the path of a falling mass, a spinning flywheel, a whirling pendulum, or a wire under tension — stored mechanical energy releases suddenly and without warning if a support fails.

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Hazard Categories in This Lab
docs.text: Category Typical source Hazard level Examples in this guide Falling mass Flywheel falling-mass method Impact injury to feet/hands; crush hazard Flywheel Rotating machinery Spinning flywheels, discs, rotors Entanglement, impact from an unbalanced or uncontained rotor Flywheel, Torsional Pendulum, Rotor Balancing Workshop Wire under tension Loaded wires (Searle's, static torsion) Whiplash injury if a stressed wire snaps Searle's Apparatus, Static Torsion Swinging/whirling bodies Pendulums, bifilar bars, Barton's pendulums Impact injury if struck by a swinging mass Bifilar Suspension, Compound Pendulum, Coupled/Forced Pendulums Sharp/fine glassware Capillary tubes, viscometer jars Cuts from broken fine-bore glass Poiseuille's Method, Capillary Rise Chemical/liquid handling Glycerine, mercury (historically), test liquids Skin/eye irritation; mercury toxicity if used Stokes' Method, Poiseuille's Method High-frequency electrical/acoustic Ultrasonic interferometer, NDT probes Electric shock from RF driver circuits; not for unsupervised student contact with live electronics Ultrasonic Interferometer, Ultrasonic NDT Workshop Structural test loads Beam load testing rigs Crush/impact hazard if an overloaded beam fails suddenly Structural Beam Load Testing Workshop

docs.title: Impact and Crush Injuries
docs.text: Falling masses (as in the flywheel experiment), swinging pendulum bobs, and suddenly-released stored elastic energy (a snapped wire, an over-tensioned spring) can all cause significant impact injury if a person is struck. Heavy flywheels and rotor-balancing rigs additionally pose an entanglement risk if loose clothing, hair, or jewellery comes into contact with a spinning shaft.

docs.title: Wire and Spring Failure
docs.text: Wires loaded close to or beyond their elastic limit (in Searle's apparatus or the static torsion experiment) can snap suddenly, releasing stored elastic energy as a rapid, whip-like recoil. Springs under significant tension or compression (coupling springs, isolator mounts) pose a similar, if usually smaller-scale, hazard if a hook or clamp gives way.

docs.title: Glassware and Fine-Bore Capillaries
docs.text: The narrow-bore capillary tubes used in the viscosity and surface-tension experiments are fragile and can shatter under even modest mechanical stress, producing sharp fragments and, if the tube was filled with liquid, a spill of that liquid as well.

docs.title: Chemical Exposure
docs.text: Test liquids such as glycerine are generally low-hazard but can still irritate skin and eyes on prolonged or repeated contact; any historical use of mercury (in barometric or manometric pressure-head arrangements) carries serious toxicity and specialized disposal requirements, and mercury-based apparatus should be handled only under appropriate institutional safety protocols, or preferably avoided altogether in favour of modern non-toxic alternatives.

docs.title: Electrical Hazards
docs.text: The ultrasonic interferometer and any real ultrasonic NDT test equipment involve radio-frequency driver electronics; live circuitry should never be handled by students without supervision, and the equipment should always be switched off and unplugged before any physical adjustment beyond the intended micrometer/reflector control.

docs.title: General Precautions
docs.text:
• Read the full procedure for any real physical apparatus before switching it on or releasing any stored energy (falling mass, spinning flywheel, tensioned wire or spring).
  • Keep hands, hair, loose clothing and jewellery well clear of any rotating or swinging component while it is in motion.
  • Never exceed the rated load of a wire, beam, or spring; always load and unload gradually and watch for any sign of the elastic limit being approached (non-proportional strain, unusual sound, or visible deformation).
  • Wear safety glasses when working with fine glassware, wires under tension, or any experiment where sudden mechanical failure could eject fragments or debris.
  • Ensure rotating machinery (flywheels, rotor-balancing rigs) is properly guarded or contained, and never touch a spinning component directly.
  • Treat all workshop rigs (structural load testing, rotor balancing) as capable of causing serious injury if operated outside their intended, supervised procedure — never attempt to exceed a rig's rated test load without explicit authorization.
  • Report any damaged wire, cracked glassware, worn cord, or unusual vibration/noise to the instructor immediately rather than continuing to use the apparatus.

This safety module, like the interactive simulator itself, is intended to build sound laboratory habits; it is not a substitute for institutional safety training or the specific risk assessment required before handling any real physical apparatus.

docs.title: 16. Ballistic Pendulum — Measuring an Unknown Launch Speed
docs.text:
Module

Practical Applications · Experiment 16

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the launch speed of a projectile (ball) using a ballistic pendulum, by combining conservation of momentum during the (inelastic) impact with conservation of energy during the subsequent swing.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
A ballistic pendulum is a simple but historically important device (originally used to measure musket-ball speeds) for finding the speed of a fast-moving projectile that is too quick to time directly over a short distance. A projectile of mass m, moving horizontally with speed v, embeds itself in a much larger stationary pendulum block of mass M suspended by a light rod or string of length L. The collision is perfectly inelastic — the combined mass (M+m) moves off together immediately after impact — so only momentum, not kinetic energy, is conserved during the collision itself.

Once the combined mass is moving, the problem becomes an ordinary energy-conservation one: the kinetic energy of the combined mass immediately after impact is entirely converted into gravitational potential energy as the pendulum swings up through some maximum angle θ, momentarily coming to rest at the top of its swing before falling back. This two-stage analysis — momentum conservation for the sudden, violent collision, followed by energy conservation for the comparatively gentle swing — is a classic technique that appears throughout mechanics whenever a fast, irreversible interaction is followed by a slower, conservative one, from vehicle-crash reconstruction to the recoil analysis of firearms.

Because the rise height h (or, equivalently, the swing angle θ) can be measured far more easily and precisely than a very short time-of-flight, the ballistic pendulum converts a hard "how fast?" measurement into an easy "how high?" one.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Momentum conservation during impact (perfectly inelastic collision):

\[ mv = (M+m)v' \]

Energy conservation during the swing (v′ is the speed immediately after impact):

\[ \tfrac{1}{2}(M+m)v'^{2} = (M+m)gh \quad\Rightarrow\quad v' = \sqrt{2gh} \]

Combining both stages, the launch speed in terms of directly measurable quantities:

\[ v = \dfrac{M+m}{m}\sqrt{2gh}, \qquad 1-\cos\theta = \dfrac{m^{2}v^{2}}{2gL(M+m)^{2}} \]

where \(m\) is the projectile mass, \(M\) the pendulum block mass, \(h\) the vertical rise of the block after impact, \(L\) the pendulum length, and \(\theta\) the maximum swing angle (so that \(h = L(1-\cos\theta)\)).

Spring-Gun Launcher Suspended Block (M) Angle Scale / Ratchet Pawl Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A spring-loaded launcher fires a small ball horizontally into a suspended, catching pendulum block; a ratchet pawl holds the block at its maximum swing angle for reading off an angle scale.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Launch speed v 2 – 8 m/s Ball (projectile) mass m 5 – 50 g Pendulum block mass M 200 – 1000 g Pendulum length L 20 – 60 cm

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Maximum swing angle θ_max ° Rise height h cm Reconstructed launch speed (check) v (calc) m/s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Ballistic Pendulum panel; set the ball mass m, block mass M and pendulum length L to fixed values for a given trial series.
  2. Adjust the launch speed slider v (representing a different spring-gun setting or cartridge) and record the resulting swing angle θ_max and rise height h.
  3. Repeat for at least five different values of the ball mass m, holding M, L and v fixed, tabulating h each time.
  4. For each trial, reconstruct the launch speed from your recorded h using the governing formula and compare it with the slider's actual value — this is the built-in consistency check for the method.
  5. Note how h changes as you vary each control in turn, to build physical intuition for the formula's dependence on m, M and L.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Variation of rise height with ball mass, at fixed launch speed v, block mass M and length L

S. No. m (g) M (g) L (cm) θ_max (°) h (cm) Reconstructed v (m/s) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Rise height, h (y-axis) against Ball mass, m (x-axis).

Expected trend: an increasing, slightly curved trend — a heavier ball carries more momentum at the same launch speed, so it swings the block-plus-ball combination higher, though the effect saturates once m becomes comparable to M.

h vs m, used to visualize how momentum transfer scales with projectile mass

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Ball mass, m (g) Rise height, h (cm) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. For each trial, compute h from the observed swing angle θ_max using \(h = L(1-\cos\theta_{max})\).
  2. Using h, m, M and L, compute the reconstructed launch speed v from the governing formula.
  3. Compare the reconstructed v with the slider's actual value in each trial and compute the percentage discrepancy.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Launch speed determined by the ballistic pendulum method

Quantity Mean value Unit 1 2

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: v = [(M+m)/m]·√(2gh) depends on h only through a square root, so h contributes half its fractional error, while the mass ratio (M+m)/m contributes its own fractional errors directly.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Ensure the ball embeds fully and instantaneously in the block (a perfectly inelastic collision) rather than bouncing off or passing through.
  • Read the maximum swing angle at the highest point of the very first swing, before any energy is lost to air resistance or pivot friction on subsequent swings.
  • Keep the pendulum's initial position exactly vertical (θ=0) before each trial, and make sure the ball's velocity is horizontal at the moment of impact.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. During the collision between the ball and the block, which quantity is conserved?
  • A) Kinetic energy only
  • B) Momentum only
  • C) Both momentum and kinetic energy
  • D) Neither

Answer: B — The collision is perfectly inelastic (the ball embeds in the block), so kinetic energy is lost to deformation/heat, but momentum is always conserved in any collision, inelastic or not.

2. If the pendulum block's mass M is made very much larger than the ball's mass m (all else fixed), the rise height h will:
  • A) Increase without bound
  • B) Approach zero
  • C) Stay exactly the same
  • D) Become negative

Answer: B — As M→∞ with m and v fixed, v′=mv/(M+m)→0, so essentially none of the ball's kinetic energy is transferred to the (now nearly immovable) block, and h→0.

3. Why is a ballistic pendulum useful for measuring speeds that are too fast to time directly?
  • A) It converts a hard "how fast" measurement into an easier "how high" (or "what angle") measurement
  • B) It stops time itself
  • C) It only works for slow projectiles
  • D) It measures speed using a stopwatch on the ball's flight

Answer: A — The swing height/angle can be read at leisure after the ball has already stopped moving relative to the block, unlike the projectile's original brief flight.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why must momentum conservation and energy conservation be applied in two separate stages here, rather than one energy-conservation equation for the whole process?
  2. What physically happens to the "missing" kinetic energy that is not accounted for by energy conservation during the collision itself?
  3. How would the result change if the ball only partially embedded and continued moving after passing through the block?
  4. Why is it important to release the ball with a purely horizontal velocity, exactly at the pendulum's rest height?
  5. Give one real-world application of the general technique (fast-collision-then-observe-the-slow-aftermath) used here.
  6. How would friction at the pendulum's pivot bias your calculated launch speed — would it read too high or too low?

docs.title: 1. Bifilar Suspension — Moment of Inertia of a Rectangular Bar
docs.text:
Module

Rotational Dynamics · Module 1

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the moment of inertia of a rectangular bar about a vertical axis through its centre, using the torsional oscillations of a bifilar suspension.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
A bifilar suspension consists of a rigid horizontal bar hung from a fixed support by two parallel, inextensible threads of equal length, attached symmetrically at equal distances on either side of the bar's centre. When the bar is rotated slightly about the vertical axis through its centre and released, it executes torsional (yawing) oscillations in a horizontal plane while both threads remain taut.

As the bar twists through a small angle, each thread traces out a small cone about the vertical, and the vertical components of the thread tensions supply a restoring torque proportional to the angular displacement — so the motion is simple harmonic, exactly analogous to a torsion pendulum but with the 'spring constant' supplied entirely by gravity acting through the geometry of the suspension rather than by a twisting wire.

This makes the bifilar suspension a convenient, wire-free method for finding the moment of inertia of any object that can be clamped to the bar (aircraft and rocket engineers have historically used scaled-up versions of exactly this method to find the moment of inertia of real airframes and control surfaces about their yaw axis).

Because the restoring torque depends on the square of the half-separation b between the threads and inversely on the thread length l, both the geometry of the suspension and the object's own mass distribution enter the period — which is what allows the moment of inertia to be extracted from a purely timing measurement.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Period of torsional oscillation:

\[ T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I\,l}{M g b^{2}}} \]

Moment of inertia:

\[ I = \dfrac{M g b^{2} T^{2}}{4\pi^{2} l} \]

where \(M\) is the mass of the bar, \(l\) the length of each thread, \(b\) the half-separation between the threads, and \(T\) the period of torsional oscillation.

Rigid Support Two Parallel Threads Suspended Bar Protractor Scale Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A rigid bar suspended by two parallel vertical threads of equal length from a rigid horizontal support, free to oscillate torsionally about the vertical axis through its centre.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Thread length l 40 – 160 cm Half-separation of threads b 5 – 25 cm Mass of bar M 100 – 600 g

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Period of oscillation T s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Bifilar Suspension panel and note the default thread length, half-separation and bar mass.
  2. Holding b and M fixed, vary the thread length l over its full range and record the period T shown on the readout for each setting.
  3. Tabulate T² against l and verify that the points lie on a straight line through the origin, confirming T² ∝ l.
  4. Now hold l fixed and vary the half-separation b; observe and note how the period changes.
  5. From the slope of the T² vs l graph (at fixed b, M), calculate the moment of inertia I of the bar using the governing formula.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Variation of period with thread length (b, M held constant)

S. No. l (cm) b (cm) M (g) Period T (s) — observed T² (s²) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot T² (y-axis) against Thread length, l (x-axis).

Expected trend: a straight line through the origin (T² ∝ l), since T² = (4π²I / Mgb²)·l.

T² vs l, expected to be a straight line through the origin

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image T² vs Thread Length Thread length, l (cm) T² (s²) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Compute the slope of the T² vs l graph: slope = 4π²I / (M g b²).
  2. Rearrange to find I = slope × M g b² / 4π², using the fixed values of M and b used in that run.
  3. Repeat with a different fixed b (holding l fixed while sweeping b, or using the b-sweep data) as a cross-check on I.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Moment of inertia of the bar about the vertical central axis

Trial Slope of T² vs l (s²/cm) M (g) b (cm) I (g·cm²) 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: I = Mgb²T²/(4π²l) is a product/quotient of measured quantities, with b and T entering squared — their fractional errors are doubled before summing.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep the angular amplitude of oscillation small so that the small-angle (simple-harmonic) approximation used in deriving the formula remains valid.
  • Ensure both threads remain taut and parallel throughout the swing — a slack thread invalidates the restoring-torque derivation.
  • Time a large number of oscillations (not just one) and divide, to reduce the fractional timing error.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. If the thread length l is doubled while b and M stay fixed, the period T:
  • A) Doubles
  • B) Increases by a factor of √2
  • C) Halves
  • D) Stays the same

Answer: B — T ∝ √l, so doubling l increases T by a factor of √2.

2. Doubling the half-separation b (all else fixed) causes the period T to:
  • A) Double
  • B) Halve
  • C) Stay the same
  • D) Quadruple

Answer: B — T ∝ 1/b, so doubling b halves the period — a wider suspension gives a stiffer restoring torque.

3. The restoring torque in a bifilar suspension arises fundamentally from:
  • A) The elasticity of the threads
  • B) Gravity acting through the changing geometry of the two threads as the bar twists
  • C) Air resistance
  • D) Magnetic effects

Answer: B — Unlike a torsion wire, the threads themselves do not twist elastically — the restoring torque comes purely from gravity via the geometric coupling of the two-thread suspension.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why must the two threads be exactly parallel and equal in length before starting the experiment?
  2. How would the period change if the bar's mass were doubled but its length (hence moment of inertia per unit mass) stayed the same?
  3. Derive, in outline, why the restoring torque is proportional to the angular displacement for small twists.
  4. What practical advantage does the bifilar method have over a torsional pendulum using a single twisted wire?
  5. How would you extend this method to find the moment of inertia of an irregularly shaped object?
  6. Why is it important to time many oscillations rather than a single one?

docs.title: 11. Capillary Rise Method — Surface Tension of a Liquid
docs.text:
Module

Fluid Properties: Viscosity & Surface Tension · Module 3

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the surface tension of a liquid by measuring the height to which it rises in a capillary tube of known bore.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
The surface of a liquid behaves as though covered by a thin, stretched elastic membrane under tension, because molecules at the surface experience a net inward force from their neighbours below (there being no liquid molecules above to balance the attraction) — this surface tension is what makes small drops nearly spherical and allows some insects to walk on water.

When a narrow glass capillary tube is dipped vertically into a wetting liquid (one that makes an acute contact angle with glass, ideally close to zero, such as water), the liquid surface inside the tube curves into a concave meniscus. The surface tension acting all around the circumference of this curved meniscus produces a net upward force on the liquid column, pulling it up the tube until this force is exactly balanced by the weight of the raised liquid column.

Equating the upward pull (surface tension × circumference × cosine of the contact angle) to the weight of the raised column (cross-sectional area × height × density × g) gives a simple relation between the rise height, the tube's bore, and the surface tension — showing that narrower tubes produce a proportionately greater rise, which is why the effect is called capillarity (from the Latin for 'hair-like').

Because the rise height is inversely proportional to the tube radius, this experiment is most cleanly performed with several capillaries of different, precisely known bores, so that a graph of h against 1/r gives a straight line whose slope directly yields the surface tension, independent of any single, possibly imprecise measurement of r.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Height of capillary rise (contact angle ≈ 0, fully wetting liquid):

\[ h = \dfrac{2T}{r\rho g} \]

Surface tension:

\[ T = \dfrac{h\, r\, \rho\, g}{2} \]

where \(r\) is the internal radius of the capillary and \(\rho\) the density of the liquid.

Liquid Beaker Fine Capillary Tube Travelling Microscope Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A set of fine glass capillary tubes of different, precisely known bores, held vertically with their lower ends dipped in a beaker of the liquid under study, observed with a travelling microscope to read the meniscus height.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Capillary radius r 0.10 – 1.00 mm Liquid (density) ρ 780 – 1300 kg/m³

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Height of capillary rise h cm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Capillary Rise panel; select a liquid and note its density.
  2. Vary the capillary radius r over the available range and record the height of rise h shown on the readout for each capillary.
  3. Plot h against 1/r and verify that the points lie on a straight line through the origin.
  4. Repeat with a different liquid and compare the slope of the resulting graph.
  5. Calculate the surface tension T from the slope of the h vs 1/r graph, using the known ρ.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Height of capillary rise for capillaries of decreasing bore

S. No. r (mm) 1/r (mm⁻¹) ρ (kg/m³) Rise height h (cm) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Rise height, h (y-axis) against 1/r (x-axis).

Expected trend: a straight line through the origin (h ∝ 1/r).

h vs 1/r, expected to be a straight line through the origin; slope = 2T/ρg

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Rise Height vs 1/r 1/r (mm⁻¹) Rise height, h (cm) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Compute the slope of the h vs 1/r graph: slope = 2T/(ρg).
  2. Rearrange to find T = ρg × slope / 2, using the known ρ.
  3. Repeat for a second liquid as a cross-check and compare the two computed surface tensions against their expected relative order.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Surface tension of the liquid(s) studied

Liquid ρ (kg/m³) Slope (cm·mm) T (dyne/cm) 1 2

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: Both h and r are lengths measured with a travelling microscope, so their combined fractional error is usually small — but the meniscus curvature must be read to the same point (bottom of meniscus) consistently to avoid a systematic bias.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Use a clean, grease-free capillary and liquid; even trace contamination changes the contact angle and surface tension significantly.
  • Ensure the capillary is held exactly vertical, since any tilt increases the effective rise height along the tube without a corresponding increase in vertical height.
  • Read the bottom of the concave meniscus with a travelling microscope, focusing precisely rather than estimating by eye.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. If the capillary radius r is halved (all else fixed), the height of capillary rise h:
  • A) Halves
  • B) Doubles
  • C) Stays the same
  • D) Quadruples

Answer: B — h ∝ 1/r, so halving the radius doubles the rise height — narrower tubes produce proportionately taller rises.

2. Capillary rise occurs because:
  • A) The liquid is boiling
  • B) Surface tension acting around the curved meniscus produces a net upward force that lifts the liquid column until balanced by its weight
  • C) Gravity pulls the liquid upward in narrow tubes
  • D) Air pressure inside the tube is higher than outside

Answer: B — The curved surface (meniscus) under tension pulls the liquid up the tube; equilibrium is reached when this pull exactly balances the weight of the raised column.

3. A liquid that does not wet glass (contact angle greater than 90°, such as mercury) will, in a capillary tube, show:
  • A) The same rise as water
  • B) A depression (fall) below the level outside the tube
  • C) No change in level at all
  • D) An enormously larger rise than water

Answer: B — For a non-wetting liquid the meniscus curves the opposite way (convex), and the surface-tension force pulls the liquid down inside the tube relative to the level outside — mercury famously shows capillary depression.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why does a wetting liquid rise in a capillary tube while a non-wetting liquid (like mercury) is depressed?
  2. Derive, in outline, the relation h = 2T/(rρg) by balancing forces on the raised liquid column.
  3. Why is it advantageous to use several capillaries of different bore and plot h vs 1/r, rather than relying on a single measurement?
  4. What effect would a greasy or contaminated capillary have on the observed rise, and why?
  5. How does temperature generally affect the surface tension of a liquid?
  6. Why must the capillary be held exactly vertical during the measurement?

docs.title: 4. Compound (Bar) Pendulum — Acceleration due to Gravity and Radius of Gyration
docs.text:
Module

Rotational Dynamics · Module 1

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the acceleration due to gravity g and the radius of gyration k of a uniform bar pendulum, using the method of conjugate points.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
A compound (or physical) pendulum is any rigid body free to oscillate about a fixed horizontal axis that does not pass through its centre of gravity — unlike the idealised simple pendulum, its mass is distributed rather than concentrated at a point. A uniform metre bar with a series of holes drilled along its length, suspended from a knife edge through any one hole, is the standard laboratory realisation.

For small oscillations, the period depends on the distance h from the pivot to the centre of gravity and on the body's radius of gyration k about an axis through the centre of gravity parallel to the pivot axis. Crucially, the same period T is obtained for two different pivot points — one on each side of the centre of gravity — whenever their distances h₁ and h₂ from the centre satisfy h₁h₂ = k². These two points are called conjugate points, and the distance between them, L = h₁ + h₂, is exactly the length of the simple pendulum that would have the same period T.

This equivalence is the basis of Kater's reversible pendulum, historically one of the most accurate methods ever devised for measuring g, since L can be measured directly with a scale (avoiding the need to separately know the body's mass distribution), while g follows from the simple-pendulum formula T = 2π√(L/g).

Once g is known (or assumed from a standard value), the same data — the period as a function of pivot distance h — can be inverted to extract the radius of gyration k, characterising how the bar's mass is distributed about its centre.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Period about a pivot at distance h from the centre of gravity:

\[ T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{k^{2}+h^{2}}{g h}} \]

Conjugate points (equal period on either side of the C.G.):

\[ h_{1}h_{2} = k^{2}, \qquad L = h_{1}+h_{2} \]

Acceleration due to gravity:

\[ g = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} L}{T^{2}} \]

Radius of gyration: \(k = \sqrt{h_{1}h_{2}}\)

Knife-Edge Support Drilled Bar Stopwatch Timing Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A uniform metal bar with a series of equally spaced holes along its length, suspended from a fixed knife-edge support through any chosen hole so it can swing freely in a vertical plane.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Pivot distance from C.G. h 5 – 45 cm Bar length l_bar 80 – 120 cm

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Period T s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Compound Pendulum panel and set the bar length to its default value.
  2. Sweep the pivot distance h from small values near the centre of gravity out to near the end of the bar, recording the period T at each setting.
  3. Plot T against h; the curve is U-shaped with a minimum, and for any horizontal line drawn above the minimum there are two intersection points — the conjugate points.
  4. Identify a pair of conjugate points (h₁ on one side, h₂ on the other) with matching periods, and compute L = h₁ + h₂.
  5. Using the common period T of the conjugate points, compute g from the simple-pendulum-equivalent formula, and k from h₁h₂ = k².

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Period as a function of pivot distance from the centre of gravity

S. No. h (cm) Period T (s) T² (s²) 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Period, T (y-axis) against Pivot distance, h (x-axis).

Expected trend: a U-shaped curve with a minimum, symmetric about the true centre of gravity when h is allowed to be negative on the other side.

T vs h — locate conjugate points as equal-T pairs on either side of the minimum

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Period vs Pivot Distance Pivot distance, h (cm) Period, T (s) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. From the T vs h graph, identify at least two pairs of conjugate points with matching T.
  2. For each pair, compute L = h₁ + h₂ and the corresponding g = 4π²L/T².
  3. Average the values of g obtained from different conjugate pairs, and similarly compute k = √(h₁h₂) for each pair.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Acceleration due to gravity and radius of gyration

Conjugate pair h₁ (cm) h₂ (cm) T (s) g (cm/s²) k (cm) 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: g = 4π²L/T² depends on T squared, so timing precision (usually via timing many oscillations) dominates the error budget more than the length measurement L.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Ensure the knife edge is horizontal and the bar swings in a single vertical plane, without any side-to-side wobble.
  • Keep the angular amplitude small (a few degrees) so the small-angle approximation underlying the SHM formula remains valid.
  • Locate conjugate points carefully — the period curve is fairly flat near the minimum, so small timing errors can shift the apparent location of matching-T points significantly there; prefer pairs well away from the minimum.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. At the two conjugate points on either side of the centre of gravity, the compound pendulum has:
  • A) Different periods but the same k
  • B) The same period, for that pair of pivot distances
  • C) Zero period
  • D) Infinite period

Answer: B — Conjugate points are defined precisely as the pair of pivot distances (one on each side of the C.G.) that give the same period.

2. As the pivot approaches the centre of gravity (h → 0), the period T:
  • A) Approaches zero
  • B) Approaches a finite minimum value
  • C) Diverges to infinity
  • D) Stays exactly constant

Answer: C — T = 2π√((k²+h²)/gh) → ∞ as h → 0, because the restoring torque (∝ h) vanishes while the moment of inertia does not — physically, a pivot exactly at the C.G. gives no gravitational restoring torque at all.

3. The compound pendulum method for measuring g has a practical advantage over a simple pendulum because:
  • A) It requires no timing at all
  • B) The equivalent length L can be measured directly and accurately as a distance along the bar, without needing to locate the pendulum's effective centre of oscillation by any other means
  • C) It does not obey Newton's laws
  • D) It is always faster to perform

Answer: B — This is exactly the insight behind Kater's reversible pendulum — L is a directly measurable geometric distance, side-stepping uncertainty in the internal mass distribution.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why does the compound pendulum have a minimum period at some pivot distance rather than at the centre of gravity itself?
  2. What physically defines a pair of conjugate points, and why must L = h₁+h₂ equal the equivalent simple pendulum length?
  3. How is Kater's reversible pendulum related to this experiment, and why was it historically important?
  4. Why would timing 20 or 50 oscillations, rather than one, substantially improve the accuracy of g?
  5. How would the T vs h curve change if the bar were replaced by one of the same length but different mass distribution (e.g. with added end masses)?
  6. What would happen to the period if the bar were pivoted exactly at its centre of gravity?

docs.title: 12. Coupled Pendulums — Normal Modes and Beats
docs.text:
Module

Oscillations, Damping & Acoustic Methods (PG) · Module 4

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the normal modes of two identical pendulums coupled by a light spring, and to observe and interpret the phenomenon of beats arising from energy transfer between them.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
Two identical simple pendulums, each of length l, connected near their bob by a light coupling spring, form a system with two degrees of freedom and hence two natural (normal) modes of oscillation, each with its own characteristic frequency. In the symmetric mode, both pendulums swing exactly in phase, with the same amplitude at every instant; the coupling spring is never stretched or compressed at all, so this mode has exactly the same frequency as either pendulum would have on its own, f_s = (1/2π)√(g/l).

In the antisymmetric mode, the two pendulums swing exactly out of phase (mirror images of each other at every instant); the spring is now alternately stretched and compressed as they swing, adding an extra restoring force on top of gravity, so this mode has a higher frequency, f_a = (1/2π)√(g/l + 2k/m), where k is the spring constant and m the bob mass.

Any other initial condition — most strikingly, releasing just one pendulum from rest while the other hangs still — can be represented as an equal superposition of these two normal modes. Because the two modes have slightly different frequencies, they gradually drift out of phase with each other as time passes; the result is that the energy of oscillation appears to slosh back and forth between the two pendulums with a characteristic beat period, even though no energy is lost overall — the first pendulum gradually comes to rest as the second one, initially still, swings up to the full original amplitude, and then the process reverses.

This transfer-of-energy phenomenon, purely a consequence of two nearby but distinct normal-mode frequencies beating against each other, is a direct mechanical analogue of phenomena as varied as coupled LC oscillator circuits, vibrational energy transfer between coupled molecular bonds, and the coupled-cavity behaviour of some laser systems.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Symmetric (in-phase) mode frequency:

\[ f_{s} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}} \]

Antisymmetric (out-of-phase) mode frequency:

\[ f_{a} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}+\dfrac{2k}{m}} \]

Beat period (energy-transfer period between the two pendulums):

\[ T_{\text{beat}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{a}-f_{s}} \] Rigid Support Pendulum 1 Coupling Spring Pendulum 2 Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). Two identical simple pendulums of equal length hung from the same rigid horizontal support, with a light spring connecting the two bobs near their midpoints to provide the coupling.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Pendulum length (both) l 20 – 100 cm Coupling spring constant k 0.5 – 5.0 N/m Bob mass (each) m 50 – 300 g

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Symmetric mode frequency f_s Hz Antisymmetric mode frequency f_a Hz Beat period T_beat s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Coupled Pendulums panel; set the pendulum length l and bob mass m to their default values.
  2. With the coupling spring constant k at its minimum (weakest coupling), note the readout values of f_s, f_a and T_beat.
  3. Increase k in equal steps, holding l and m fixed, and tabulate f_s, f_a and T_beat at each step.
  4. Verify that f_s stays constant (independent of k) while f_a increases with k, exactly as the formulas predict.
  5. Plot T_beat against k and observe how strengthening the coupling shortens the beat period — energy transfers back and forth more rapidly as the coupling gets stronger.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Normal mode frequencies and beat period with increasing coupling strength

S. No. l (cm) k (N/m) m (g) f_s (Hz) f_a (Hz) T_beat (s) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Beat period, T_beat (y-axis) against Coupling spring constant, k (x-axis).

Expected trend: a monotonically decreasing curve — stronger coupling transfers energy between the pendulums more rapidly, shortening the beat period.

T_beat vs k, expected to decrease monotonically as coupling strengthens

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Beat Period vs Coupling Constant Coupling spring constant, k (N/m) Beat period, T_beat (s) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Verify that the measured f_s matches (1/2π)√(g/l) using the set pendulum length, independent of k.
  2. Verify that the measured f_a matches (1/2π)√(g/l + 2k/m) for each value of k and m used.
  3. Compute T_beat = 1/(f_a − f_s) for each trial and compare with the readout value.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Normal mode frequencies and beat behaviour

l (cm) k (N/m) f_s (Hz) f_a (Hz) T_beat, calculated (s) T_beat, observed (s) 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: Because T_beat depends on the small difference (f_a − f_s), even modest uncertainties in either frequency are amplified when this difference is small (weak coupling).

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Release both pendulums from rest with only one displaced, to cleanly excite the beating pattern rather than a general mixture of modes.
  • Keep oscillation amplitudes small so both pendulums remain in the simple-harmonic regime throughout.
  • Ensure both pendulums are of exactly equal length, since any asymmetry mixes the two normal modes and complicates the simple beat picture.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. In the symmetric normal mode of two identical coupled pendulums, the coupling spring:
  • A) Stretches and compresses maximally
  • B) Is never stretched or compressed, since both pendulums move identically
  • C) Breaks
  • D) Oscillates at twice the pendulum frequency

Answer: B — Since both pendulums swing exactly in phase with equal amplitude at every instant, the distance between their bobs (and hence the spring's length) never changes.

2. The antisymmetric mode always has a higher frequency than the symmetric mode because:
  • A) The antisymmetric mode has a shorter effective pendulum length
  • B) The coupling spring provides an additional restoring force only when the pendulums move out of phase
  • C) It doesn't — they are always equal
  • D) The antisymmetric mode ignores gravity

Answer: B — In the antisymmetric mode the spring is alternately stretched and compressed, contributing an extra restoring force on top of gravity, which raises the frequency above f_s = (1/2π)√(g/l).

3. If the coupling spring constant k is increased (all else fixed), the beat period T_beat:
  • A) Increases
  • B) Decreases
  • C) Stays exactly the same
  • D) Becomes negative

Answer: B — Stronger coupling increases f_a while f_s stays fixed, widening the frequency difference (f_a−f_s) and hence shortening T_beat = 1/(f_a−f_s).

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Explain physically why releasing just one pendulum excites an equal superposition of both normal modes.
  2. Why does the symmetric mode frequency not depend on the coupling spring constant at all?
  3. Derive, in outline, the beat period in terms of the two normal-mode frequencies.
  4. What would happen to the beating pattern if the two pendulums had noticeably different lengths?
  5. Name at least one other physical system (electrical, molecular, or optical) that shows an analogous normal-mode beating phenomenon.
  6. How would friction/damping, if included, eventually affect the long-term behaviour of this coupled system?

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the decay of amplitude in a damped harmonic oscillator, and to determine the logarithmic decrement and quality factor characterising the damping.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
Every real oscillator loses energy over time to friction, air resistance, or other dissipative mechanisms, so its oscillations gradually die away rather than continuing forever at constant amplitude, as an ideal (undamped) simple-harmonic oscillator would. For the common case of 'light' damping — where the dissipative force is proportional to velocity and not too large — the amplitude decays exponentially with time while the system still oscillates at a frequency only very slightly lower than its undamped natural frequency.

The logarithmic decrement, δ, defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of any two successive amplitude peaks, provides a simple, dimensionless measure of how quickly the oscillation dies away: a larger δ means faster decay (heavier damping) per cycle. Because the amplitude ratio between successive peaks is the same throughout the entire decay for exponential damping, δ can equally well be measured from peaks that are several cycles apart, then divided by the number of intervening cycles, to reduce the effect of any single reading error.

A closely related and widely used figure of merit is the quality factor, Q, which for light damping is well approximated by Q ≈ π/δ; physically, Q is (up to a factor of 2π) the number of radians of oscillation the system completes before its energy decays to 1/e of its initial value, so a high-Q oscillator (a good pendulum clock, a struck tuning fork, or a superconducting microwave cavity) rings for a very long time, while a low-Q oscillator (a car's suspension, deliberately) dies away within a cycle or two.

This same mathematics of exponential amplitude decay, logarithmic decrement, and quality factor recurs throughout physics and engineering — from mechanical vibration damping and electrical RLC circuits to the linewidth of atomic spectral lines and the ringdown of laser cavities — making this simple mechanical experiment a gateway to a very general and important concept.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Exponential amplitude decay:

\[ A_{n} = A_{0}\, e^{-\gamma t}, \qquad \gamma = \dfrac{b}{2m} \]

Logarithmic decrement (from successive peak amplitudes):

\[ \delta = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_{n}}{A_{n+1}}\right) = \gamma T \]

Quality factor (light damping):

\[ Q \approx \dfrac{\pi}{\delta} \] Mass-Spring System Adjustable Damper Amplitude Scale Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A mass–spring (or torsional) oscillator fitted with an adjustable dashpot or eddy-current damper, together with a means of recording the amplitude of successive oscillations (e.g. an attached pointer and scale, or a motion sensor).

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Damping coefficient b 0.05 – 1.00 kg/s Oscillating mass m 50 – 300 g Spring/restoring constant k_spring 5 – 40 N/m

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Logarithmic decrement δ dimensionless Quality factor Q dimensionless

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Damped Oscillations panel; set the mass m and spring constant k_spring to their default values.
  2. Starting from the smallest damping coefficient b, note the logarithmic decrement δ and quality factor Q shown on the readout.
  3. Increase b in equal steps, holding m and k_spring fixed, and tabulate δ and Q at each step.
  4. Plot δ against b and verify the expected roughly linear increase; plot Q against b and verify the expected decrease.
  5. From the slope of the δ vs b graph, estimate the period T of the (lightly damped) oscillation, and compare with T = 2π√(m/k_spring).

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Logarithmic decrement and quality factor with increasing damping

S. No. b (kg/s) m (g) k_spring (N/m) δ (log. decrement) Q (quality factor) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Logarithmic decrement, δ (y-axis) against Damping coefficient, b (x-axis).

Expected trend: an increasing, roughly linear trend (δ = bT/2m, with T only weakly dependent on b for light damping).

δ vs b, expected to increase roughly linearly for light-to-moderate damping

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Logarithmic Decrement vs Damping Coefficient Damping coefficient, b (kg/s) Logarithmic decrement, δ 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Compute the slope of the δ vs b graph: slope ≈ T/2m.
  2. Rearrange to estimate the period T ≈ 2m × slope, and compare with the expected T = 2π√(m/k_spring).
  3. For each trial, verify Q ≈ π/δ against the readout value of Q.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Damping characteristics of the oscillator

b (kg/s) δ Q T estimated (s) T expected (s) 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: Since δ is a logarithm of an amplitude ratio, its absolute error is approximately the sum of the fractional errors in the two amplitude readings — reading amplitudes several cycles apart and dividing by the cycle count reduces this error.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Read amplitude peaks on the same side of the equilibrium position each time (e.g. only the rightward extremes), to avoid any small asymmetry in the setup biasing the result.
  • Where possible, measure the amplitude ratio between peaks separated by several cycles and divide by the number of intervening cycles, rather than relying on a single successive pair.
  • Keep the initial amplitude small enough that damping can be treated as linear (proportional to velocity) throughout the run.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. The logarithmic decrement δ is defined as:
  • A) The ratio of the period to the amplitude
  • B) The natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes
  • C) The total number of oscillations before stopping
  • D) The undamped natural frequency

Answer: B — δ = ln(A_n/A_{n+1}) by definition, and for exponential decay this ratio (and hence δ) is the same for every successive pair of peaks.

2. A high-quality-factor (high-Q) oscillator is one that:
  • A) Stops oscillating almost immediately
  • B) Continues oscillating for many cycles before its energy decays significantly
  • C) Has no restoring force
  • D) Oscillates at zero frequency

Answer: B — High Q corresponds to light damping — the system 'rings' for a long time, completing many radians of oscillation before its energy falls to 1/e of its initial value.

3. If the damping coefficient b is increased (all else fixed), the logarithmic decrement δ:
  • A) Decreases
  • B) Increases
  • C) Stays exactly constant
  • D) Becomes negative

Answer: B — δ = γT = (b/2m)T, so increasing b (which increases the decay rate γ) increases δ.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why is the ratio of successive amplitudes constant throughout an exponentially damped oscillation, rather than the difference?
  2. Derive, in outline, the approximate relation Q ≈ π/δ for light damping.
  3. Why might you prefer to measure amplitudes several cycles apart rather than just two successive peaks?
  4. How would critically damped or overdamped motion differ qualitatively from the lightly damped case studied here?
  5. Give an example of a real system where a high Q is desirable, and one where a low Q is deliberately engineered.
  6. How does the period of a lightly damped oscillator compare with the period of the same system if it were undamped?

docs.title: 3. Flywheel — Moment of Inertia and Frictional Couple
docs.text:
Module

Rotational Dynamics · Module 1

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the moment of inertia of a flywheel about its own axis, and the frictional couple opposing its rotation, by the falling-mass method.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
A flywheel is a heavy wheel mounted on a horizontal axle so that it can rotate with very little friction. A cord is wound several times around the axle and a known mass m is attached to its free end. As the mass falls through a height h, it unwinds the cord and sets the flywheel spinning; when the cord finally leaves the axle (after n₁ complete turns), the flywheel continues to spin under its own inertia, gradually decelerating due to friction at the bearings and air resistance, and comes to rest after a further n₂ revolutions.

Energy conservation over the falling phase relates the mass's loss of potential energy to the kinetic energy gained by both the falling mass and the spinning flywheel, plus the work done against the (assumed constant) frictional torque over that same phase. The deceleration phase, in which the flywheel alone loses all its rotational kinetic energy to friction over n₂ revolutions, gives a second, independent equation for the frictional torque.

Combining the two phases eliminates the unknown frictional torque and yields the flywheel's moment of inertia I purely in terms of the falling mass, axle radius, height of fall, angular velocity at detachment, and the numbers of revolutions n₁ and n₂ — quantities that are all directly measurable, without needing to measure friction independently.

This experiment nicely illustrates that friction, far from being a mere nuisance to be minimised, can be measured and incorporated quantitatively into an energy-balance calculation — a technique used throughout rotating machinery design, from turbine rotors to hard-disk spindles.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Angular velocity when the cord leaves the axle (n₁ turns in time t):

\[ \omega = \dfrac{2\pi n_{1}}{t} \]

Moment of inertia of the flywheel:

\[ I = \dfrac{m r^{2}\left(2 g h - \omega^{2} r^{2}\right)}{\omega^{2}}\times\dfrac{n_{2}}{n_{1}+n_{2}} \]

Frictional couple:

\[ \tau_{f} = \dfrac{I\,\omega}{2\pi n_{2}\,t_{2}} \times 2\pi \;=\; \dfrac{I\,\omega^{2}}{4\pi n_{2}} \]

where \(m\) is the falling mass, \(r\) the axle radius, \(h\) the height of fall, \(t\) the fall time, \(n_1\) the number of cord turns on the axle, and \(n_2\) the number of extra revolutions made by the flywheel after the cord detaches, before it stops.

Flywheel on Axle Wound Cord Falling Mass Revolution Counter Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A heavy wheel free to rotate on a nearly frictionless horizontal axle, with a cord wound around the axle carrying a falling mass over a pulley or directly off the axle, and a marker to count revolutions.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Falling mass m 50 – 300 g Axle radius r 0.5 – 2.5 cm Height of fall h 50 – 150 cm Cord turns on axle n₁ 5 – 15 turns

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Time of fall t s Extra revolutions to stop n₂ rev

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Flywheel panel; wind the cord (set n₁) and choose a falling mass m, axle radius r, and height of fall h.
  2. Release the mass (conceptually) and record the fall time t shown on the readout, along with the number of extra revolutions n₂ the flywheel makes before stopping.
  3. Repeat for at least five different values of the falling mass m, keeping r, h and n₁ fixed, tabulating t and n₂ each time.
  4. For each trial compute ω = 2πn₁/t, then calculate I using the governing formula.
  5. Verify that the calculated I remains reasonably constant across trials with different m — this is the internal consistency check for this method.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Variation of fall time and stopping revolutions with falling mass

S. No. m (g) h (cm) t (s) n₂ (rev) ω = 2πn₁/t (rad/s) I (g·cm²) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot 1/t² (y-axis) against Falling mass, m (x-axis).

Expected trend: an increasing, roughly linear trend — larger falling masses accelerate the system faster, shortening the fall time.

1/t² vs m, used to cross-check the consistency of the calculated I

Overlay opacity Clear pasted image 1/t² vs Falling Mass Falling mass, m (g) 1/t² (s⁻²) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. For each trial, compute ω and then I from the formula above.
  2. Take the mean of I over all trials as the best estimate of the flywheel's moment of inertia.
  3. Using the mean I, compute the frictional couple τ_f for each trial and take its mean.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Moment of inertia and frictional couple of the flywheel

Quantity Mean value Unit 1 2

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: The axle radius r and fall time t both enter squared in the governing formula, so their fractional errors are doubled before summing with the others.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Wind the cord evenly and tightly, in a single layer, so the effective axle radius r does not change during unwinding.
  • Make sure the cord detaches cleanly from the axle at the end of n₁ turns rather than slipping or tangling.
  • Count revolutions during the deceleration phase carefully; this count (n₂) is just as important as the timed fall itself.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. If the axle radius r is doubled (all else fixed), the angular velocity ω at cord detachment, for the same fall time t, is:
  • A) Doubled
  • B) Halved
  • C) Unchanged, since ω = 2πn₁/t does not involve r directly
  • D) Quadrupled

Answer: C — ω depends only on the number of turns n₁ and the time t, not directly on r — though a different r will generally change t itself for a given falling mass and height.

2. The frictional couple in this experiment is determined from:
  • A) A separate direct measurement with a spring balance
  • B) The deceleration phase, where the flywheel's rotational KE is dissipated entirely by friction over n₂ revolutions
  • C) Assuming friction is exactly zero
  • D) The colour of the flywheel

Answer: B — The whole point of the n₂ (post-detachment) measurement is to let friction alone be responsible for stopping the flywheel, giving an energy-balance equation for the frictional torque.

3. If the flywheel had no friction at all, what would n₂ be?
  • A) Zero
  • B) Infinite — it would never stop
  • C) Exactly equal to n₁
  • D) Undefined

Answer: B — With zero friction there is nothing to remove the flywheel's rotational kinetic energy, so it would continue spinning indefinitely.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why is the falling-mass method able to determine both I and the frictional torque without an independent friction measurement?
  2. What assumption is made about the frictional torque during the two phases of the motion, and how reasonable is it?
  3. How would you experimentally locate the moment when the cord just leaves the axle?
  4. Why is it important to measure n₂ (extra revolutions) as well as the fall time t?
  5. How would the result change if the cord were wound in more than one layer?
  6. Why do larger flywheels (larger I) take longer to both spin up and spin down?

docs.title: 14. Forced Oscillations and Resonance — Barton's Pendulums
docs.text:
Module

Oscillations, Damping & Acoustic Methods (PG) · Module 4

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the steady-state response of a damped oscillator to a periodic driving force, and to determine the resonant frequency and quality factor from the resonance curve.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
When a damped oscillator is driven by an external periodic force of frequency ω, rather than left to oscillate freely, it eventually settles into a steady state in which it oscillates at exactly the driving frequency ω (not at its own natural frequency ω₀), with an amplitude and phase lag relative to the driving force that both depend sensitively on how close ω is to ω₀ and on the amount of damping present.

Barton's pendulums is the classic demonstration of this: a heavy 'driver' pendulum is set swinging, and its motion is coupled (via a stretched string) to a row of lighter pendulums of various lengths hung from the same string. Each lighter pendulum experiences the same driving frequency (that of the driver), but the one whose own natural frequency ω₀ most closely matches the driving frequency swings with by far the largest amplitude — visually and dramatically demonstrating resonance.

The steady-state amplitude, as a function of driving frequency, traces out a characteristic peaked curve (the resonance curve): it is small for driving frequencies far from ω₀, rises to a maximum near ω = ω₀, and the sharpness of this peak — the ratio of the resonant frequency to the width of the peak at half its maximum height — is precisely the quality factor Q introduced in the damped-oscillation experiment, now measured in an entirely different (frequency-domain) way. Lightly damped systems (high Q) show a very sharp, tall resonance peak; heavily damped systems (low Q) show a broad, shallow one.

Resonance is far from a mere laboratory curiosity: it governs the tuning of radio receivers, the design (and avoidance of unwanted resonance in) bridges and buildings under wind or seismic loading, magnetic resonance imaging, and the vibration behaviour of virtually every mechanical structure subjected to periodic loads.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Steady-state amplitude of a driven, damped oscillator:

\[ A(\omega) = \dfrac{F_{0}/m}{\sqrt{\left(\omega_{0}^{2}-\omega^{2}\right)^{2} + \left(2\gamma\omega\right)^{2}}} \]

Phase lag between response and driving force:

\[ \phi = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2\gamma\omega}{\omega_{0}^{2}-\omega^{2}}\right) \]

Quality factor from the resonance curve (sharpness of the peak):

\[ Q = \dfrac{f_{0}}{\Delta f} \]

where \(\Delta f\) is the full width of the resonance curve at half its maximum power (amplitude²).

Driver Pendulum Coupling String Barton's Pendulums Variable-Frequency Drive Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A heavy 'driver' pendulum coupled via a taut string to a row of lighter pendulums of different lengths (Barton's pendulums), together with a variable-frequency drive and an amplitude/phase read-out for the pendulum of interest.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Driving frequency f_drive 0.2 – 3.0 Hz Damping (of driven pendulum) γ 0.05 – 1.00 s⁻¹ Natural frequency of driven pendulum f0 0.5 – 2.0 Hz

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Steady-state amplitude A cm Phase lag φ degrees

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Forced Oscillations & Resonance panel; set the natural frequency f0 and damping γ of the driven pendulum to their default values.
  2. Sweep the driving frequency f_drive across a range spanning below and above f0, and record the steady-state amplitude A and phase lag φ at each setting.
  3. Plot A against f_drive; the curve should peak sharply near f_drive = f0.
  4. Determine the full width Δf of the resonance peak at half its maximum power (amplitude²), and calculate Q = f0/Δf.
  5. Repeat the frequency sweep for a larger damping γ and compare the resulting (now broader, shorter) resonance curve and its Q.

docs.text:
Table: Steady-state amplitude and phase lag as the driving frequency is swept through resonance

S. No. f_drive (Hz) Amplitude A (cm) Phase lag φ (°) 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Amplitude, A (y-axis) against Driving frequency, f_drive (x-axis).

Expected trend: a peaked curve, maximum near f_drive = f0, narrower and taller for lower damping.

A vs f_drive — the resonance curve; locate the peak and its half-power width

Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Resonance Curve Driving frequency, f_drive (Hz) Amplitude, A (cm) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. From the A vs f_drive graph, read off the peak frequency (an estimate of f0) and the peak amplitude A_max.
  2. Find the two frequencies on either side of the peak where A² = A_max²/2 (half-power points); their difference is Δf.
  3. Compute Q = f0/Δf, and compare with the Q obtained independently from the damped-oscillation (time-domain) experiment for a similar damping setting.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Resonance characteristics of the driven pendulum

γ (s⁻¹) f0 (Hz), from peak Δf (Hz) Q = f0/Δf 1 2

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: The half-power width Δf is usually the less precisely determined quantity, since it depends on locating two points on a curve that may be sparsely sampled near the half-power level.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Allow the driven pendulum to settle into its steady-state response at each driving frequency before recording the amplitude — the initial transient response is a mixture of the driving frequency and the system's own natural frequency and must be allowed to die away.
  • Sample the driving frequency more finely near the expected resonance, where the amplitude changes rapidly, than far from it.
  • Keep the driving amplitude F0 constant throughout the entire frequency sweep, so the resonance curve reflects only the frequency dependence, not a changing drive strength.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. At resonance (driving frequency equal to the natural frequency), the phase lag between the response and the driving force is approximately:
  • A) 0°
  • B) 45°
  • C) 90°
  • D) 180°

Answer: C — From the phase formula, when ω = ω₀ the denominator (ω0²−ω²) vanishes, giving φ = arctan(∞) = 90°.

2. A sharply peaked, tall resonance curve corresponds to:
  • A) Heavy damping (low Q)
  • B) Light damping (high Q)
  • C) Zero driving force
  • D) No damping information can be inferred from the curve's shape

Answer: B — Light damping allows the amplitude to build up to a very large value right at resonance while falling off quickly away from it, giving a narrow, tall peak and hence a high Q = f0/Δf.

3. The steady-state response of a driven oscillator settles at a frequency equal to:
  • A) The oscillator's own natural frequency, regardless of the driving frequency
  • B) The driving frequency, not the oscillator's natural frequency
  • C) The average of the two frequencies
  • D) Zero

Answer: B — In the steady state, the driven oscillator is forced to oscillate at exactly the frequency of the periodic driving force, even though its amplitude and phase depend on how this compares to its own natural frequency.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why does the phase lag between the driven oscillator and the driving force pass through 90° exactly at resonance?
  2. How is the quality factor obtained from a resonance curve related to the quality factor obtained from a free (damped) decay measurement?
  3. In Barton's pendulums, why does only the pendulum whose length matches the driver's length swing with a large amplitude?
  4. Why must you wait for the transient response to die away before reading a 'steady-state' amplitude at each driving frequency?
  5. Give a real engineering example where avoiding resonance is a critical design consideration.
  6. How would the resonance curve change if the damping were reduced to nearly zero?
