docs.title: 18. Gyroscopic Precession — Spin, Torque and Steady Precession
docs.text:
Module

Practical Applications · Experiment 18

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the steady precession of a rapidly spinning wheel under an applied gravitational torque, and to verify that the precession rate is inversely proportional to the spin rate.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
A rapidly spinning wheel possesses a large spin angular momentum \(L = I\omega\) directed along its axle, where I is the wheel's moment of inertia about that axle and ω its spin rate. If the wheel's centre of gravity is offset by a small distance d from its pivot point, gravity exerts a torque \(\tau = Mgd\) about the pivot, where M is the wheel's mass.

Naively, one might expect this torque to simply tip the wheel over, rotating its axle downward in the direction of the torque. Instead, because angular momentum is a vector and torque causes the angular momentum vector to change according to \(\vec{\tau} = d\vec{L}/dt\), the axle instead sweeps around slowly in a horizontal circle — a motion called precession — while the wheel continues to spin rapidly about its own axle exactly as before. This counter-intuitive behaviour is the essence of gyroscopic motion, and for a rapidly spinning wheel with a small, steady torque, the precession settles into a steady rate

\[ \Omega = \dfrac{\tau}{L} = \dfrac{Mgd}{I\omega} \]

For a disc-shaped wheel with \(I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^{2}\), the wheel's own mass M cancels out of this expression entirely, leaving \(\Omega = 2gd/(R^{2}\omega)\) — the precession rate depends only on the wheel's radius, its spin rate, and the offset distance, not on how heavy the wheel is. This single fact — that a faster-spinning gyroscope precesses more slowly and resists tipping more strongly — is exploited in every mechanical gyrocompass, in the natural self-stability of a fast-spinning bicycle or motorcycle wheel, and in the reaction wheels used to orient spacecraft without any external thruster fuel.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Spin angular momentum and applied torque:

\[ L = I\omega = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^{2}\omega, \qquad \tau = Mgd \]

Steady precession rate:

\[ \Omega = \dfrac{\tau}{L} = \dfrac{2gd}{R^{2}\omega} \]

Precession period:

\[ T_{\text{prec}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\Omega} \]

where M and R are the spinning wheel's mass and radius, ω its spin rate about its own axle, and d the offset of its centre of gravity from the pivot.

Spinning Wheel (M, R) Gimbal & Pivot Point Precession-Angle Stopwatch/Scale Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A wheel is spun up about its own axle and mounted so its centre of gravity is offset from a fixed pivot; the axle then precesses slowly around the pivot, timed with a stopwatch and angle scale.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Wheel mass M 100 – 500 g Wheel radius R 3 – 8 cm Spin rate ω 5 – 60 rad/s C.G. offset from pivot d 2 – 10 cm

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Spin angular momentum L g·cm²/s Precession rate Ω rad/s Precession period T s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Gyroscopic Precession panel; set the wheel mass M, radius R and offset d to fixed values.
  2. Vary the spin rate ω and record the resulting precession rate Ω and precession period T from the readouts.
  3. Repeat for at least five different values of ω, keeping M, R and d fixed, tabulating Ω each time.
  4. Verify that the product Ω·ω remains approximately constant across your trials (since Ω ∝ 1/ω) — this is the key experimental signature of steady gyroscopic precession.
  5. Repeat the whole sweep with a different wheel radius R, and confirm that Ω changes as 1/R² while the wheel mass M has no effect on Ω.

docs.text:

S. No. M (g) R (cm) d (cm) ω (rad/s) Ω (rad/s) Ω·ω (check, ≈ constant) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Precession rate, Ω (y-axis) against Spin rate, ω (x-axis).

Expected trend: a falling, hyperbola-like curve (Ω ∝ 1/ω) — spin the wheel faster and it precesses more slowly and more steadily.

Ω vs ω, illustrating the inverse relationship between spin and precession rates

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. For each trial, compute the spin angular momentum L = ½MR²ω and the applied torque τ = Mgd.
  2. Compute the precession rate Ω = τ/L for each trial and compare it with the simulator's readout.
  3. Verify that Ω·ω is approximately constant across your sweep of ω, confirming the inverse relationship.

Quantity Mean value Unit 1 2

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: Since Ω = 2gd/(R²ω), the wheel radius R enters squared — the least-count error of whatever instrument measures R (often a vernier caliper) usually dominates the overall uncertainty in Ω.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Bring the wheel up to a steady, constant spin rate before releasing it to precess — the steady-precession formula assumes ω is essentially unchanging over the timescale of one precession cycle.
  • Keep the offset distance d small compared with the wheel radius R, so that the "fast-spin, slow-precession" approximation used in the governing formula remains valid.
  • Support the pivot itself as friction-free as possible, since pivot friction introduces an additional torque that is not accounted for in the ideal formula.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. If the spin rate ω of the wheel is doubled (all else fixed), the precession rate Ω becomes:
  • A) Doubled
  • B) Halved
  • C) Unchanged
  • D) Quadrupled

Answer: B — Since Ω = 2gd/(R²ω), Ω is inversely proportional to ω, so doubling ω halves Ω.

2. Why does the wheel's own mass M cancel out of the precession-rate formula for a disc-shaped wheel?
  • A) Because both the spin angular momentum L and the applied torque τ are directly proportional to M, so M cancels in their ratio
  • B) Because gravity does not act on spinning objects
  • C) Because the formula is only an approximation that happens to ignore mass
  • D) It does not actually cancel; the statement is false

Answer: A — L = ½MR²ω and τ = Mgd are both proportional to M, so Ω = τ/L cancels M exactly, leaving only R, ω, d and g.

3. A fast-spinning gyroscope resists tipping over more strongly than a slow-spinning one because:
  • A) It has more kinetic energy, which somehow opposes gravity directly
  • B) Its larger angular momentum L means the same torque produces a smaller rate of change of the angular momentum's direction (a smaller Ω)
  • C) Faster spinning reduces its weight
  • D) There is no real difference; spin rate is irrelevant to stability

Answer: B — Torque changes the direction of L at a rate Ω=τ/L; a larger L (from faster spin) means the same torque redirects the angular momentum vector more slowly, i.e., the gyroscope precesses (tips) more slowly.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why does an applied torque on a spinning gyroscope cause it to precess sideways rather than simply falling over in the direction of the torque?
  2. Derive, in outline, why the precession rate Ω is inversely proportional to the spin rate ω.
  3. Why does the wheel's mass drop out of the precession formula for a disc, and would it drop out for a different mass distribution (e.g., a hoop)?
  4. Give one real engineering application where gyroscopic precession is deliberately exploited (not merely tolerated), and explain the underlying principle.
  5. How would you expect the results to change if the wheel were spun in the opposite direction?
  6. What would happen to the precession rate if the spin rate ω were allowed to slowly decay due to friction, over a long observation time?

docs.title: 5. Non-Uniform (Cantilever) Bending
docs.text: ← Back to Course Portal · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: 5. Non-Uniform (Cantilever) Bending — Young's Modulus
docs.text:
Module

Elasticity of Solids · Module 2

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the Young's modulus of the material of a beam by the method of non-uniform (cantilever) bending.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
When a beam is clamped rigidly at one end and loaded at the free end, it is called a cantilever, and the resulting bending is termed non-uniform bending because the bending moment varies continuously along the beam's length — it is greatest at the fixed end and zero at the free end where the load is applied.

Under a load, each thin longitudinal filament of the beam is either stretched (on the convex, outer side of the bend) or compressed (on the concave, inner side), while filaments along the central 'neutral axis' remain unchanged in length. The elastic restoring forces generated by this internal stretching and compression across the beam's cross-section balance the external bending moment produced by the load at every point along the beam.

Integrating this balance along the length of the cantilever gives the depression (vertical deflection) of the free end in terms of the applied load, the beam's length, its cross-sectional dimensions, and the material's Young's modulus — the deflection increases with the cube of the length, making even modest beams quite noticeably 'springy' when long.

This experiment gives one of the most direct and intuitive routes to Young's modulus, since the depression can be measured with a travelling microscope focused on a pin fixed to the loaded end, and its clean cubic dependence on length provides an excellent internal check of the theory.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Depression of the free end under a load W = Mg:

\[ y = \dfrac{4 W l^{3}}{Y b d^{3}} \]

Young's modulus:

\[ Y = \dfrac{4 W l^{3}}{b d^{3} y} \]

where \(l\) is the length of the cantilever (clamp to load point), \(b\) the breadth, \(d\) the thickness of the beam, and \(W\) the applied load.

Rigid Clamp Cantilever Beam Load Hanger Travelling Microscope Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A rectangular beam rigidly clamped at one end to a heavy stand, loaded with a hanger and slotted weights at the free end, with a pin fixed vertically at the loaded end observed through a travelling microscope to measure its depression.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Load M 50 – 500 g Cantilever length l 15 – 45 cm Beam breadth b 1.0 – 3.0 cm Beam thickness d 0.20 – 0.80 cm

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Depression of free end y mm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Non-Uniform Bending panel; set the beam breadth b, thickness d and cantilever length l to their default values.
  2. Vary the load M in equal steps and record the depression y of the free end shown on the readout at each step.
  3. Plot y against M; the points should lie on a straight line through the origin (y ∝ M for fixed geometry).
  4. Reset the load to a middle value and instead vary the cantilever length l; tabulate y for each length and verify the expected y ∝ l³ dependence by plotting y against l³.
  5. Calculate Young's modulus Y from the slope of the y vs M graph, using the known l, b and d.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Depression of the free end with increasing load (l, b, d held constant)

S. No. M (g) l (cm) b (cm) d (cm) Depression y (mm) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Depression, y (y-axis) against Load, M (x-axis).

Expected trend: a straight line through the origin (y ∝ M).

y vs M, expected to be a straight line through the origin; slope = 4gl³/(Ybd³)

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Depression vs Load Load, M (g) Depression, y (mm) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Compute the slope of the y vs M graph: slope = 4gl³/(Ybd³).
  2. Rearrange to find Y = 4gl³/(bd³ × slope), using the fixed l, b, d for that run.
  3. Separately verify the y ∝ l³ dependence from the length-sweep data and estimate Y from that graph's slope as a cross-check.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Young's modulus of the beam material

Trial l (cm) b (cm) d (cm) Slope (mm/g) Y (dyne/cm²) 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: Y = 4Wl³/(bd³y) depends on the cantilever length l cubed and on the thickness d cubed (inversely) — small errors in measuring l or, especially, the thin dimension d, are amplified threefold.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Load and unload in the same steps and average the depression for loading and unloading, to eliminate any small elastic-hysteresis or clamp-slip error.
  • Wait for oscillations of the loaded beam to die out completely before taking a microscope reading.
  • Measure the beam thickness d at several points with a screw gauge, since Y depends on d cubed and the beam may not be perfectly uniform.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. For a fixed load, if the cantilever length l is doubled, the depression y becomes:
  • A) Twice as large
  • B) Four times as large
  • C) Eight times as large
  • D) Unchanged

Answer: C — y ∝ l³, so doubling l increases y by a factor of 2³ = 8.

2. If the beam thickness d is halved (all else fixed), the depression y:
  • A) Halves
  • B) Doubles
  • C) Increases eightfold
  • D) Decreases eightfold

Answer: C — y ∝ 1/d³, so halving d increases y by a factor of 2³ = 8 — thin beams are dramatically more flexible.

3. Along a loaded cantilever, the bending moment is:
  • A) Zero everywhere
  • B) Maximum at the free end, zero at the clamp
  • C) Maximum at the clamped end, zero at the free end
  • D) Constant along the length

Answer: C — The bending moment at any section equals the load times its distance from that section to the free end, so it is greatest at the fixed end and zero exactly at the point of loading.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why is this bending called 'non-uniform', and how does that differ from the uniform bending experiment?
  2. Where along the neutral axis does a longitudinal filament experience zero strain, and why?
  3. Why does the depression depend on the cube of the length but only the cube of the thickness (inversely), rather than some other power?
  4. What experimental precautions minimise systematic error from the beam's own weight and any residual bending before loading?
  5. How would you distinguish, experimentally, whether a beam is behaving elastically (returns to zero on unloading) or has undergone some permanent set?
  6. Why is a travelling microscope, rather than a ruler, used to measure the depression?

docs.title: 9. Poiseuille's Method
docs.text: ← Back to Course Portal · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: 9. Poiseuille's Method — Coefficient of Viscosity of a Liquid
docs.text:
Module

Fluid Properties: Viscosity & Surface Tension · Module 3

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the coefficient of viscosity of a liquid by studying its steady, streamline flow through a narrow capillary tube under a constant pressure head.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
When a liquid flows slowly and smoothly (in streamline, or laminar, flow) through a narrow horizontal capillary tube under a small, steady pressure difference between its two ends, adjacent layers of the liquid slide over one another at different speeds — fastest along the central axis and effectively zero at the tube wall (the no-slip condition) — because of the liquid's internal friction, or viscosity.

Poiseuille analysed this velocity profile and showed that the volume of liquid flowing per second depends extremely sensitively on the tube's radius (to the fourth power), linearly on the pressure gradient driving the flow, and inversely on both the liquid's viscosity and the tube's length — a result now called Poiseuille's law, foundational to understanding flow in blood vessels, industrial pipelines, and microfluidic devices alike.

In this experiment, the pressure head is supplied by a column of the liquid itself, of height h above the capillary, giving a driving pressure P = ρgh; a fixed volume V of liquid is collected at the outlet and the time t taken is measured, from which the coefficient of viscosity η can be calculated directly.

Because the r⁴ dependence makes the flow rate so sensitive to the capillary's bore, accurately knowing (or independently calibrating) the capillary radius is usually the single most important practical requirement of this experiment.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Poiseuille's equation (volume V collected in time t):

\[ \eta = \dfrac{\pi P r^{4} t}{8 V l} \]

where \(P = \rho g h\) is the driving pressure (liquid density \(\rho\), head \(h\)), \(r\) the capillary radius, and \(l\) its length.

Constant-Level Reservoir Narrow Capillary Collection Vessel & Stopwatch Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A graduated reservoir maintaining a constant liquid level connected to a narrow horizontal capillary tube, with a measuring cylinder and stopwatch to time the collection of a fixed volume at the outlet.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Pressure head h 5 – 40 cm Capillary radius r 0.20 – 0.60 mm Capillary length l 5 – 20 cm Liquid selection (density) ρ 800 – 1300 kg/m³

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Time to collect fixed volume V = 10 cm³ t s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Poiseuille's Method panel; select a liquid and note its density, and set the capillary radius and length to their default values.
  2. Vary the pressure head h in equal steps and record the time t (to collect the fixed volume V) shown on the readout at each step.
  3. Plot 1/t against h and verify that the points lie on a straight line through the origin (since Q = V/t ∝ h at fixed r, l).
  4. Repeat with a different liquid (different density/viscosity) and compare the resulting graphs.
  5. Calculate the coefficient of viscosity η from the slope of the 1/t vs h graph, using the known r, l, ρ and V.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Time to collect a fixed volume of liquid, with increasing pressure head

S. No. h (cm) Volume V (cm³) Time t (s) 1/t (s⁻¹) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot 1/t (y-axis) against Pressure head, h (x-axis).

Expected trend: a straight line through the origin (1/t ∝ h).

1/t vs h, expected to be a straight line through the origin; slope = πρgr⁴/(8ηlV)

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image 1/t vs Pressure Head Pressure head, h (cm) 1/t (s⁻¹) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Compute the slope of the 1/t vs h graph: slope = πρgr⁴/(8ηlV).
  2. Rearrange to find η = πρgr⁴/(8lV × slope), using the known r, l, ρ and V.
  3. Repeat for a second liquid and compare the two computed viscosities against their expected relative order.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Coefficient of viscosity of the liquid(s) studied

Liquid ρ (kg/m³) r (mm) l (cm) Slope (s⁻¹/cm) η (poise) 1 2

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: η depends on the capillary radius to the fourth power — even a very small uncertainty in r, typically measured with a travelling microscope on a mercury thread, dominates the total error.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Maintain a constant pressure head during collection — use a constant-level (overflow) arrangement rather than letting the head fall as liquid drains.
  • Ensure the flow remains streamline (laminar) throughout — very high heads or very wide capillaries risk turbulent flow, which invalidates Poiseuille's law.
  • Measure the capillary's bore radius carefully, ideally by weighing a mercury thread of known length inside it, since this is the dominant source of error.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. If the capillary radius r is doubled (all else fixed), the flow rate Q for the same pressure head:
  • A) Doubles
  • B) Increases fourfold
  • C) Increases eightfold
  • D) Increases sixteenfold

Answer: D — Poiseuille's law gives Q ∝ r⁴, so doubling r increases the flow rate sixteenfold — this extreme sensitivity is why capillary bore must be known very precisely.

2. Poiseuille's law is strictly valid only for:
  • A) Turbulent flow
  • B) Streamline (laminar) flow
  • C) Flow of a gas only
  • D) Flow with zero viscosity

Answer: B — The derivation assumes smooth, layered (laminar) flow with no radial mixing; at high flow rates the motion becomes turbulent and the simple r⁴ law no longer applies.

3. Doubling the capillary length l (all else fixed) changes the flow rate by a factor of:
  • A) 2
  • B) 1/2
  • C) 4
  • D) 1/4

Answer: B — Q ∝ 1/l, so doubling the length halves the flow rate for the same driving pressure.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why is the flow rate through a capillary so extremely sensitive to its radius, compared to its length?
  2. What physical assumption distinguishes streamline (laminar) flow from turbulent flow, and how would you recognise experimentally that the flow has become turbulent?
  3. Why must the pressure head be kept constant during the timed collection, rather than allowed to fall as the reservoir empties?
  4. How would the required collection time change if a more viscous liquid (e.g. glycerine instead of water) were used, all else equal?
  5. Derive, in outline, why the velocity profile across the capillary is parabolic in laminar flow.
  6. What everyday or industrial situations depend critically on the r⁴ law demonstrated in this experiment?

docs.title: 17. Rolling Race on an Incline
docs.text: ← Back to Course Portal · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: 17. Rolling Race on an Incline — Moment of Inertia and Rolling Efficiency
docs.text:
Module

Practical Applications · Experiment 17

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study how the mass distribution of a rolling body (characterized by its moment-of-inertia factor k) governs its acceleration down an incline, and to verify the theoretical prediction that a solid disc or sphere always outpaces a ring or hoop released from rest at the same height.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
When a rigid body of mass m and radius r rolls without slipping down an incline of angle θ, gravity does work on its centre of mass, and that work is shared between translational kinetic energy of the centre of mass and rotational kinetic energy about the body's own axis. Writing the moment of inertia as \(I = kmr^{2}\), where k is a dimensionless shape factor (k=1 for a thin ring/hoop, k=½ for a solid disc or cylinder, k=⅖ for a solid sphere, k=⅔ for a thin hollow sphere), energy conservation for rolling without slipping gives an acceleration down the slope of

\[ a = \dfrac{g\sin\theta}{1+k} \]

Crucially, this acceleration does not depend on the body's mass or radius at all — only on its shape, through k. A larger k means more of the body's mass sits farther from the rolling axis (as in a hoop, where essentially all the mass is at the rim), so a larger fraction of the released potential energy is diverted into spinning rather than forward motion, and the body accelerates more slowly.

This is exactly why, in the classic demonstration of releasing a solid ball and a hoop from the top of a ramp at the same instant, the solid ball always wins the race — regardless of their individual masses or sizes — a fact with real engineering consequences: flywheels, vehicle wheels, and rotating drums are deliberately designed with specific mass distributions (k values) to trade off rotational inertia (useful for energy storage or smoothing) against translational responsiveness (useful for acceleration and fuel efficiency).

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Rolling acceleration down the incline:

\[ a = \dfrac{g\sin\theta}{1+k}, \qquad I = kmr^{2} \]

Time to travel a distance L along the slope, starting from rest:

\[ t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2L}{a}} \]

Speed on reaching the bottom:

\[ v = at = \sqrt{\dfrac{2gh}{1+k}}, \qquad h = L\sin\theta \]

where θ is the incline angle, L the distance travelled along the slope, and k the shape factor of the rolling body (1 for a ring, ½ for a disc/cylinder, ⅖ for a solid sphere, ⅔ for a hollow sphere).

Adjustable Incline (θ) Ring / Disc / Sphere Set Photogate / Stopwatch Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). An incline of adjustable angle, a set of interchangeable rolling bodies (ring, disc, solid sphere, hollow sphere) of similar overall size, and a timer to record the descent.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Shape (moment-of-inertia factor) k {1, 0.5, 0.4, 0.667} — Incline angle θ 10 – 40 ° Incline length (along slope) L 50 – 200 cm

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Acceleration a cm/s² Time to reach bottom t s Speed at bottom v cm/s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Rolling Race panel; select a shape (ring, disc/cylinder, solid sphere, or hollow sphere) and set the incline angle θ and slope length L.
  2. Record the time to reach the bottom, t, shown on the readout.
  3. Repeat for at least five different incline angles θ, keeping the shape and L fixed, tabulating t each time.
  4. Repeat the whole sweep for a second, different shape, and compare the two sets of times at the same angles — the shape with the larger k should consistently take longer.
  5. For each trial, compute a = 2L/t² from your timed data, then compare this experimental k (from a = g sinθ/(1+k)) against the theoretical value for that shape.

docs.text:
Table: Variation of descent time with incline angle, for a fixed rolling shape

S. No. Shape (k) θ (°) L (cm) t (s) a = 2L/t² (cm/s²) k (experimental) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Time to reach bottom, t (y-axis) against Incline angle, θ (x-axis).

Expected trend: a decreasing curve for every shape — steeper inclines shorten the descent — with the ring/hoop's curve lying consistently above the disc's or sphere's curve at every angle.

t vs θ, compared across at least two different rolling shapes

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Incline angle, θ (°) Time to reach bottom, t (s) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. For each trial, compute the experimental acceleration a = 2L/t² from your timed data.
  2. From a = g sinθ/(1+k), solve for the experimental k and compare it with the theoretical value for that shape.
  3. Compute the percentage deviation between your experimental and theoretical k for each shape tested.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Experimental moment-of-inertia factor k for each rolling shape tested

Quantity Mean value Unit 1 2

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: The moment-of-inertia factor k is inferred from the measured acceleration a = g sinθ/(1+k); since a is obtained from the timed run (a = 2L/t²), the fall time t enters squared and usually dominates the error budget.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Release each rolling body from rest with no initial push, and from exactly the same starting point on the incline for every trial.
  • Ensure genuine rolling without slipping throughout — a very smooth/oily incline surface can let the body slide rather than roll, invalidating the energy-conservation formula used here.
  • Use rolling bodies of similar overall size so that air resistance and rolling friction remain comparably small across the shapes being compared.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. Between a solid sphere and a solid disc released from the same height on the same incline, which reaches the bottom first?
  • A) The disc
  • B) The sphere
  • C) They arrive at exactly the same time
  • D) It depends on their masses

Answer: B — The sphere has the smaller k (⅖ vs ½), so more of its energy goes into translation and less into rotation, giving it a larger acceleration.

2. Why does the rolling acceleration a = g sinθ/(1+k) not depend on the mass or radius of the rolling body?
  • A) Because mass and radius cancel out of the energy-conservation equation when I is written as kmr²
  • B) Because gravity does not act on rotating bodies
  • C) Because friction exactly cancels the effect of mass
  • D) It does depend on them; the formula is only approximate

Answer: A — Both the translational and rotational kinetic energy terms are proportional to m (and, via I=kmr², to r² as well), so these factors cancel completely, leaving a that depends only on θ and the shape factor k.

3. A hollow cylinder (thin pipe) and a solid cylinder of the same outer radius are released together. Which one reaches the bottom first?
  • A) The hollow cylinder
  • B) The solid cylinder
  • C) Both arrive together
  • D) Neither moves

Answer: B — The hollow cylinder has all its mass concentrated near the rim (higher k, closer to that of a ring), so more of its energy is diverted into rotation, making it slower than the solid cylinder (k=½).

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why is "rolling without slipping" essential to the formula used here, and what would go wrong if the body slid instead?
  2. Explain, in terms of energy sharing, why a hoop always loses a rolling race to a solid disc of the same mass and radius.
  3. How could you experimentally verify that a given ball is solid rather than hollow, using only this incline and a stopwatch?
  4. Why is the mass of the rolling body absent from the final formula for acceleration, even though gravity clearly depends on mass?
  5. Name one piece of real machinery (a flywheel, a vehicle wheel, an industrial roller) where the designer's choice of k has a genuine practical consequence, and explain why.
  6. How would rolling friction (a small resistive torque at the contact point) bias your experimentally determined k — too high or too low?

docs.title: 7. Searle's Apparatus
docs.text: ← Back to Course Portal · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: 7. Searle's Apparatus — Young's Modulus by Direct Elongation
docs.text:
Module

Elasticity of Solids · Module 2

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the Young's modulus of the material of a wire by directly measuring its elongation under a known tensile load, using Searle's apparatus.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
Searle's apparatus consists of two identical, long wires of the same material hung side by side from the same rigid support, each carrying a frame at its lower end. One wire (the 'reference' wire) carries a fixed, steady load throughout, keeping it taut; the other (the 'experimental' wire) additionally carries a hanger onto which further known weights are added.

A sensitive spirit-level, pivoted between the two frames with a micrometer screw at one end, detects the tiny relative extension of the experimental wire compared to the reference wire — any elongation tilts the level, which is restored to horizontal by turning the micrometer screw, whose reading then directly gives the elongation. Using a second, unstretched reference wire hung in parallel automatically cancels out any common effects such as temperature-induced expansion or slow sagging of the support, which would otherwise be indistinguishable from the elongation being measured.

Because the extension of a wire under simple tension is directly proportional to the applied load, and inversely proportional to its cross-sectional area, this experiment gives Young's modulus in perhaps its most direct textbook form — literally 'stress divided by strain' — with stress = Mg/(πr²) and strain = extension/original length.

This differential, two-wire design is the same basic principle used in far more sensitive strain-gauge and interferometric elongation measurements in materials-testing laboratories today.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Extension under load Mg:

\[ l_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{M g L}{\pi r^{2} Y} \]

Young's modulus:

\[ Y = \dfrac{M g L}{\pi r^{2}\, l_{\text{ext}}} \]

where \(L\) is the original (unstretched) length of the experimental wire and \(r\) its radius.

Rigid Support Reference Wire + Frame Experimental Wire + Load Spirit Level & Micrometer Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). Two identical wires of the same material hung side by side from a rigid support, each ending in a frame; a spirit level pivoted between the frames with a micrometer screw detects the relative extension of the loaded (experimental) wire against the steadily-loaded reference wire.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Load added M 200 – 2000 g Original wire length L 100 – 300 cm Wire radius r 0.20 – 0.60 mm

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Elongation l_ext mm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Searle's Apparatus panel; set the wire length L and radius r to their default values.
  2. Add load M in equal steps and record the elongation shown on the readout (the micrometer reading needed to restore the spirit level to horizontal) at each step.
  3. Plot the elongation against the load M; verify it is a straight line through the origin.
  4. Unload in the same steps and verify the readings retrace the same line (no permanent set), confirming the wire remained within its elastic limit.
  5. Calculate Young's modulus from the slope of the elongation vs load graph, using the known L and r.

docs.text:

S. No. Load M (g) Micrometer reading — loading (mm) Micrometer reading — unloading (mm) Mean elongation (mm) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.text:
Plot Elongation (y-axis) against Load, M (x-axis).

Elongation vs M, expected to be a straight line through the origin; slope = gL/(πr²Y)

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Compute the slope of the elongation vs M graph: slope = gL/(πr²Y).
  2. Rearrange to find Y = gL/(πr² × slope), using the known L and r.
  3. Check that the loading and unloading data agree within experimental scatter — divergence indicates the elastic limit was exceeded.

docs.text:

L (cm) r (mm) Slope (mm/g) Y (dyne/cm²) 1

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: The wire radius r, measured with a screw gauge, enters squared and is typically sub-millimetre — its fractional error usually dominates the overall uncertainty in Y.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Remove any kinks in the wire beforehand and apply an initial small load to both wires to keep them taut and straight before starting readings.
  • Always turn the micrometer screw in the same direction when approaching the level (horizontal) position, to avoid backlash error.
  • Load and unload gradually and note both sets of readings, checking for any permanent set that would indicate the elastic limit was exceeded.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. The purpose of the second (reference) wire in Searle's apparatus is to:
  • A) Provide a backup in case the experimental wire breaks
  • B) Cancel out common effects such as temperature expansion and support sag that would otherwise be mistaken for elongation
  • C) Double the measured extension
  • D) Reduce the total load

Answer: B — Because both wires hang from the same support and experience the same temperature and any support movement identically, only the differential extension — the true elastic elongation — is registered by the spirit level.

2. Doubling the wire radius r (all else fixed) changes the elongation for the same load by a factor of:
  • A) 2
  • B) 1/2
  • C) 4
  • D) 1/4

Answer: D — Elongation ∝ 1/r², so doubling r reduces the elongation to 1/4 of its original value.

3. Backlash error in the micrometer screw is minimised by:
  • A) Turning the screw rapidly
  • B) Always approaching the final (level) reading from the same rotational direction
  • C) Using a thicker wire
  • D) Ignoring the screw reading entirely

Answer: B — Backlash (small mechanical play) in a screw thread causes a different effective zero depending on the direction of the last turn — always finishing in the same direction removes this systematic error.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why does Searle's apparatus use two wires rather than one, and what specific systematic errors does this design eliminate?
  2. Derive the relation between stress, strain and Young's modulus, and identify each in this specific experiment.
  3. Why is it important to remove kinks and apply an initial small load before beginning the actual measurement?
  4. What is backlash error in a micrometer screw, and how is it avoided in this experiment?
  5. How would you verify, purely from your own data, that the wire remained within its elastic limit throughout?
  6. Why is the wire radius, rather than its length, usually the dominant source of error in the final result for Y?

docs.title: 8. Static Torsion — Rigidity Modulus of a Wire by the Static Twist Method
docs.text:
Module

Elasticity of Solids · Module 2

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the rigidity (shear) modulus of the material of a wire by measuring its static angle of twist under a known applied couple.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
Unlike the torsional pendulum, which infers the rigidity modulus from a dynamic (oscillating) measurement, the static torsion method measures it directly and statically: one end of the wire is clamped rigidly, while the other end is attached to a horizontal disc or wheel fitted with a pulley of known radius. A cord wound around the pulley and passed over a frictionless guide carries a weight pan, so that a known torque (couple) can be applied to the wire, twisting it through a measurable angle.

For an isotropic elastic wire obeying Hooke's law in shear, the angle of twist per unit applied torque is a constant, called the torsional compliance, that depends on the wire's rigidity modulus, its length, and — very sensitively — its radius (to the fourth power), exactly as in the dynamic torsional-pendulum case, since both ultimately derive from the same relation between shear stress, shear strain and the twist gradient along the wire.

By applying a series of known torques and reading the corresponding twist angle on a graduated circular scale (using a pointer or lamp-and-scale arrangement for high precision), a straight-line graph of twist angle against applied torque can be obtained; its slope gives the torsional compliance and hence the rigidity modulus directly, without any timing measurement at all.

This static method complements the torsional-pendulum method: it avoids any assumptions about a symmetric mass distribution or oscillatory damping, but places correspondingly greater demands on precisely reading small angles.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Static twist under an applied couple C:

\[ \theta = \dfrac{2 C l}{\pi \eta r^{4}} \]

Rigidity modulus:

\[ \eta = \dfrac{2 C l}{\pi r^{4} \theta} \]

where the applied couple \(C = F R_{\text{arm}}\) (\(F\) = weight applied, \(R_{\text{arm}}\) = pulley radius), \(l\) the length of the wire between the clamp and the disc, and \(r\) its radius. \(\theta\) is measured in radians.

Rigid Clamp Wire Disc with Pulley Cord, Weights & Circular Scale Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A wire clamped rigidly at the top and attached at the bottom to a horizontal disc fitted with a pulley of known radius; a cord over the pulley carries a weight pan, and a pointer with a graduated circular scale reads the resulting twist angle.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Applied weight F 50 – 500 g Pulley (arm) radius R_arm 2 – 8 cm Wire length l 30 – 100 cm Wire radius r 0.20 – 0.80 mm

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Angle of twist θ degrees

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Static Torsion panel; set the pulley radius, wire length and wire radius to their default values.
  2. Increase the applied weight F in equal steps and record the twist angle θ shown on the readout at each step.
  3. Plot θ against the applied couple C = F·R_arm; verify a straight line through the origin.
  4. Unload in the same steps and check for any residual twist (permanent set) once the load is fully removed.
  5. Calculate the rigidity modulus η from the slope of the θ vs C graph, using the known l and r.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Twist angle with increasing applied couple

S. No. F (g) R_arm (cm) C = F·R_arm (g·cm) θ (°) θ (rad) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Twist angle, θ (y-axis) against Applied couple, C (x-axis).

Expected trend: a straight line through the origin (θ ∝ C).

θ vs C, expected to be a straight line through the origin; slope = 2l/(πηr⁴)

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Twist Angle vs Applied Couple Applied couple, C (g·cm) Twist angle, θ (rad) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Compute the slope of the θ (in radians) vs C graph: slope = 2l/(πηr⁴).
  2. Rearrange to find η = 2l/(πr⁴ × slope), using the known l and r.
  3. Check for and note any residual (non-zero) twist angle after full unloading, which would indicate the elastic limit was exceeded.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Rigidity modulus of the wire material

l (cm) r (mm) Slope (rad per g·cm) η (dyne/cm²) 1

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: As in the torsional pendulum, the wire radius r enters raised to the fourth power, so its measurement error (from a screw gauge) dominates the total uncertainty in η by far.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Wind the cord over the pulley without slipping and ensure it leaves the pulley horizontally so the effective arm radius is exactly R_arm.
  • Take readings only after the twist has settled and any oscillation has died out.
  • Do not exceed the elastic limit of the wire — if a residual twist remains after full unloading, discard the run and repeat with smaller loads.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. If the pulley (arm) radius R_arm is doubled while the applied weight F stays the same, the applied couple C:
  • A) Doubles
  • B) Halves
  • C) Stays the same
  • D) Quadruples

Answer: A — C = F·R_arm, so doubling the arm radius doubles the couple for the same weight.

2. The static torsion method differs from the torsional pendulum method chiefly in that it:
  • A) Uses a different material
  • B) Measures the twist directly under a steady applied torque rather than inferring the torsional constant from an oscillation period
  • C) Cannot determine the rigidity modulus at all
  • D) Requires no wire

Answer: B — Both methods find the same torsional constant C = πηr⁴/2l, but the static method measures θ directly under a known steady torque instead of timing oscillations.

3. A residual twist remaining after the load is fully removed indicates that:
  • A) The wire behaved perfectly elastically
  • B) The elastic limit of the wire was exceeded during the run
  • C) The pulley radius was measured incorrectly
  • D) Nothing is wrong; this is expected

Answer: B — A perfectly elastic wire returns exactly to zero twist on full unloading; any residual twist is evidence of permanent (plastic) deformation.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Derive, in outline, why the twist angle is directly proportional to the applied couple for an elastic wire.
  2. Why does the twist angle depend on the fourth power of the wire radius?
  3. What experimental precautions ensure that the applied couple is accurately F·R_arm and nothing else?
  4. How would you check, using only your own loading/unloading data, whether the wire remained within its elastic limit?
  5. Compare the static torsion method with the torsional pendulum method for determining η — what are the relative advantages of each?
  6. Why is it important for the cord to leave the pulley exactly horizontally?

docs.title: 10. Stokes' Method — Viscosity by Terminal Velocity of a Falling Sphere
docs.text:
Module

Fluid Properties: Viscosity & Surface Tension · Module 3

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the coefficient of viscosity of a highly viscous liquid by measuring the terminal velocity of small spheres falling freely through it.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
When a small sphere falls through a viscous fluid, it experiences three forces: its weight acting downward, the upward buoyant (upthrust) force due to the displaced fluid, and a viscous drag force that opposes its motion and increases with speed. Sir George Stokes showed that for a sphere falling slowly enough that the flow around it remains laminar, this drag force is directly proportional to the sphere's radius, its speed, and the fluid's viscosity — a result now called Stokes' law.

Immediately after release the sphere accelerates, since its weight initially exceeds the (still small) sum of buoyancy and drag; but as its speed increases, the drag force grows until the three forces exactly balance, at which point the sphere continues to fall at a constant speed called its terminal velocity. Because this terminal velocity depends on the sphere's radius and density and on the fluid's density and viscosity, measuring it gives a direct route to the fluid's viscosity if the other quantities are known.

In this experiment, small spheres of a known material (hence known density) and various radii are dropped, one at a time, into a tall glass jar of the liquid under study, and the time taken to fall a marked distance well below the point where terminal velocity is reached is measured. Repeating for several sphere sizes and plotting the terminal velocity against the square of the radius gives a straight line whose slope yields the viscosity.

Highly viscous liquids such as glycerine or castor oil are typically studied by this method, since terminal velocity is reached quickly and can be sustained over an easily measurable distance, unlike in a low-viscosity liquid such as water where a sphere would reach the bottom of the jar before ever attaining a genuinely steady terminal speed.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Stokes' law — terminal velocity:

\[ v = \dfrac{2}{9}\, \dfrac{r^{2} g (\rho-\sigma)}{\eta} \]

Coefficient of viscosity:

\[ \eta = \dfrac{2}{9}\, \dfrac{r^{2} g (\rho-\sigma)}{v} \]

where \(r\) is the sphere's radius, \(\rho\) its density, \(\sigma\) the density of the liquid, and \(v\) the terminal (constant) velocity.

Tall Liquid Jar Falling Sphere Marked Rings & Stopwatch Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A tall, wide glass jar filled with the liquid under study, with two marked rings on the outside a known distance apart, well below the surface, and a mechanism for gently releasing small spheres one at a time along the central axis.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Sphere radius r 0.5 – 3.0 mm Sphere material (density) ρ 2700 – 8900 kg/m³ Liquid (density) σ 1000 – 1300 kg/m³

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Terminal velocity v cm/s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Stokes' Method panel; select a sphere material and a liquid.
  2. Vary the sphere radius r over the available range and record the terminal velocity v shown on the readout for each size.
  3. Plot v against r² and verify that the points lie on a straight line through the origin.
  4. Repeat for a different liquid (different density/viscosity) and compare the slope of the resulting graph.
  5. Calculate the coefficient of viscosity η from the slope of the v vs r² graph, using the known ρ, σ.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Terminal velocity for spheres of increasing radius

S. No. r (mm) r² (mm²) ρ (kg/m³) Terminal velocity v (cm/s) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Terminal velocity, v (y-axis) against r² (x-axis).

Expected trend: a straight line through the origin (v ∝ r²).

v vs r², expected to be a straight line through the origin; slope = 2g(ρ−σ)/9η

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Terminal Velocity vs r² r² (mm²) Terminal velocity, v (cm/s) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Compute the slope of the v vs r² graph: slope = 2g(ρ−σ)/9η.
  2. Rearrange to find η = 2g(ρ−σ)/(9 × slope), using the known ρ and σ.
  3. Repeat for a different sphere material or liquid as a cross-check, and compare the two independently obtained values of η.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Coefficient of viscosity of the liquid

Liquid σ (kg/m³) Sphere material ρ (kg/m³) Slope (cm/s per mm²) η (poise) 1 2

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: The sphere radius enters squared, and since ρ and σ are usually close in value for typical sphere/liquid combinations, the small difference (ρ−σ) can itself carry a relatively large fractional uncertainty.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Drop each sphere gently along the central axis of the jar, well clear of the walls, since wall proximity introduces an extra drag correction not accounted for in the simple Stokes' law formula.
  • Mark the timing points well below the point of release, to be certain terminal velocity has genuinely been reached before timing begins.
  • Use small spheres and a sufficiently viscous liquid so the flow around the sphere remains laminar (low Reynolds number), as Stokes' law assumes.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. A sphere falling through a viscous liquid reaches terminal velocity when:
  • A) Its weight becomes zero
  • B) The net force on it becomes zero, i.e. weight equals the sum of buoyancy and drag
  • C) The drag force becomes zero
  • D) It touches the bottom of the jar

Answer: B — Terminal velocity is precisely the constant speed at which the three forces (weight, buoyancy, drag) balance, giving zero net force and zero acceleration.

2. If the sphere's radius is doubled (all else fixed), Stokes' law predicts the terminal velocity:
  • A) Doubles
  • B) Increases fourfold
  • C) Increases eightfold
  • D) Halves

Answer: B — v ∝ r², so doubling r increases the terminal velocity by a factor of 4.

3. Why is this method suited to highly viscous liquids rather than water?
  • A) Stokes' law is only mathematically valid for viscous liquids
  • B) In a low-viscosity liquid the sphere reaches the bottom of the jar before ever settling into a steady terminal velocity that can be measured
  • C) Water is too dense to use
  • D) Spheres float in water

Answer: B — High viscosity keeps the terminal velocity low and the settling distance to reach it short, giving a long, easily measurable stretch of genuinely constant-velocity fall — this is impractical in a low-viscosity liquid like water.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. State Stokes' law and identify the three forces that act on a falling sphere in a viscous liquid.
  2. Why must the sphere be released well above the marked timing points?
  3. What correction becomes necessary if the sphere's diameter is not small compared to the diameter of the containing jar?
  4. How would the terminal velocity change if a sphere of the same radius but greater density were used?
  5. Why does this method work well for glycerine but poorly for water?
  6. What is the Reynolds number, and why does Stokes' law require it to be small?

docs.title: 2. Torsional Pendulum — Rigidity Modulus and Moment of Inertia by the Two-Body Method
docs.text:
Module

Rotational Dynamics · Module 1

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the rigidity (shear) modulus of the material of a suspension wire and the moment of inertia of a circular disc, using the two-body method of a torsional pendulum.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
A torsional pendulum consists of a heavy circular disc suspended from a rigid support by a thin wire fixed rigidly at both ends. When the disc is rotated in its own horizontal plane through a small angle and released, the wire, twisted through that angle, exerts a restoring torque proportional to the twist (Hooke's law in shear), so the disc executes angular simple harmonic motion.

The proportionality constant relating restoring torque to twist angle — the torsional rigidity C of the wire — depends on the wire's rigidity (shear) modulus η, its length l and, very sensitively, its radius r (as the fourth power), since C = πηr⁴/2l. A thin wire is therefore very much 'softer' torsionally than a thick one of the same material and length.

The period of oscillation depends on both C and the disc's own moment of inertia I about the wire's axis: T = 2π√(I/C). Since I is generally not known beforehand for an arbitrary disc, the two-body method sidesteps this by placing two identical, equal point masses symmetrically on the disc at a known distance d from its centre. This adds a known increment 2md² to the moment of inertia without changing C, giving a second, longer period T' whose difference from the first period T lets I be eliminated and calculated purely from measured quantities.

Once I is found this way, the same period T (measured with the disc alone) yields C, and hence the rigidity modulus η of the wire — one of the two fundamental elastic constants of an isotropic solid, alongside Young's modulus.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Torsional constant of the wire:

\[ C = \dfrac{\pi \eta r^{4}}{2l} \]

Period with disc alone / with two added masses:

\[ T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I}{C}}, \qquad T' = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I + 2md^{2}}{C}} \]

Two-body method — moment of inertia of the disc:

\[ I = \dfrac{2 m d^{2} T^{2}}{T'^{2} - T^{2}} \]

Rigidity modulus:

\[ \eta = \dfrac{8\pi l I}{r^{4} T^{2}} \] Rigid Clamp Suspension Wire Circular Disc Symmetric Slot Masses Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A heavy circular disc suspended by a thin wire clamped rigidly at the top, free to execute torsional oscillations in its own plane; symmetric slots allow two equal masses to be clamped at known radii for the two-body method.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Wire length l 30 – 100 cm Wire radius r 0.20 – 0.80 mm Added-mass distance from axis d 4 – 12 cm Each added mass m 50 – 250 g

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Period, disc alone T s Period, with two added masses T′ s

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Torsional Pendulum panel; note the default wire length, radius, and the readout period T of the bare disc.
  2. Set the added-mass distance d and mass m to their default values; note the new readout period T′ with both masses symmetrically placed.
  3. Repeat for several different values of d (keeping l, r, m fixed) and tabulate T and T′ each time.
  4. Use the two-body formula to calculate the disc's moment of inertia I for each trial and check its consistency (I should not depend on d).
  5. Using I and the bare-disc period T, calculate the rigidity modulus η of the wire from the given formula, for several values of l and r.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Two-body method: period with and without added symmetric masses

S. No. d (cm) m (g) T (s) T′ (s) I = 2md²T²/(T′²−T²) (g·cm²) 1 2 3 4 5

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot T′² − T² (y-axis) against d² (x-axis).

Expected trend: a straight line through the origin, since T′² − T² = (2m/C)·d².

(T′² − T²) vs d², expected to be a straight line through the origin

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image T² vs Added Mass Distance² d² (cm²) T′² − T² (s²) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Verify that I calculated from the two-body formula is essentially constant across different values of d — this is the key internal consistency check of the method.
  2. Using the mean I and the bare period T, compute C = 4π²I/T², then η = 2Cl/(πr⁴).
  3. Repeat for at least two different wire radii r and compare the two independently-determined values of η — they should agree within experimental error, since η is a material property independent of the wire's dimensions.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Rigidity modulus of the wire material

Trial l (cm) r (mm) I (g·cm²) η (dyne/cm²) 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: η = 8πlI/(r⁴T²) depends on r to the fourth power and T squared — even a small uncertainty in the wire radius (measured with a screw gauge) dominates the total error.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Add the two masses exactly symmetrically about the axis, at equal distances d, so no net translational imbalance or wobble is introduced.
  • Keep the twist angle small and let the disc settle into a clean, wobble-free oscillation before timing.
  • Measure the wire radius r with a screw gauge at several points along its length, since η depends on r⁴ and this is usually the dominant source of error.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. Doubling the wire radius r (all else fixed) changes the torsional constant C by a factor of:
  • A) 2
  • B) 4
  • C) 8
  • D) 16

Answer: D — C ∝ r⁴, so doubling r increases C by a factor of 2⁴ = 16 — a thicker wire is dramatically stiffer torsionally.

2. In the two-body method, why is it valid to say I is independent of the added-mass distance d used in the trial?
  • A) Because I is a property of the disc alone and does not depend on what is temporarily added to it
  • B) Because d cancels algebraically in every formula
  • C) Because the masses are massless in the idealised model
  • D) It is not independent — this is a limitation of the method

Answer: A — I refers to the disc's own moment of inertia; the added masses are external, known contributions used only to create a second data point — the recovered I should be the same regardless of which d was used.

3. If a wire twice as long but of the same radius and material is used, the period T of the same disc will:
  • A) Increase by a factor of √2
  • B) Decrease by a factor of √2
  • C) Stay the same
  • D) Double

Answer: A — C ∝ 1/l, so T = 2π√(I/C) ∝ √l — doubling l increases T by √2.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why does the rigidity modulus depend on the fourth power of the wire radius rather than, say, the square?
  2. What is the two-body method designed to eliminate, and how does it achieve this algebraically?
  3. Distinguish between Young's modulus and the rigidity (shear) modulus — what kind of deformation does each describe?
  4. Why must the two added masses be placed at exactly equal distances from the axis and be of exactly equal mass?
  5. How would you check experimentally that the wire is genuinely obeying Hooke's law throughout the twist range you used?
  6. What would happen to the period if the disc were replaced by a ring of the same mass and radius?

docs.title: 15. Ultrasonic Interferometer
docs.text: ← Back to Course Portal · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: 15. Ultrasonic Interferometer — Velocity of Sound and Elastic Constants of a Liquid (PG)
docs.text:
Module

Oscillations, Damping & Acoustic Methods (PG) · Module 4

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the velocity of ultrasonic waves in a liquid using an ultrasonic interferometer, and hence to calculate the liquid's adiabatic compressibility and bulk modulus.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
An ultrasonic interferometer generates high-frequency (megahertz-range) longitudinal sound waves in a liquid using a piezoelectric quartz crystal fixed at the base of a small measuring cell, driven by a radio-frequency oscillator. A movable, reflecting plate at the top of the liquid column can be raised or lowered very precisely using a micrometer screw, allowing the length of the liquid column between the crystal and the reflector to be adjusted continuously.

When the reflector is at a distance from the crystal equal to a whole number of half-wavelengths, the wave reflected back from it arrives in phase with the newly generated wave, setting up a stationary (standing) wave pattern in the liquid column; this resonance condition loads the crystal's electrical circuit in a way that produces a sharp, repeatable maximum in the anode (or drive) current of the oscillator, easily read off a built-in meter.

As the micrometer is turned steadily in one direction, successive current maxima occur every time the reflector moves through exactly half a wavelength, so the distance between consecutive maxima directly gives λ/2; averaging this spacing over many maxima (rather than trusting a single interval) gives a precise wavelength, and hence, since the driving frequency f is fixed and known, the velocity of sound v = fλ in the liquid.

Once v is known, and given the liquid's density ρ (measured separately, e.g. by a pyknometer), the liquid's adiabatic compressibility β = 1/(ρv²) and adiabatic bulk modulus K = ρv² follow immediately — these fundamental elastic constants, difficult to access by any purely mechanical (static) method for a liquid, are exactly what make the ultrasonic interferometer an important and widely used PG-level instrument in acoustics, chemical physics, and materials characterisation of liquids and solutions.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Wavelength from the spacing of successive current maxima:

\[ \lambda = 2\,\overline{\Delta d} \]

Velocity of ultrasonic waves in the liquid:

\[ v = f\lambda \]

Adiabatic compressibility and bulk modulus:

\[ \beta = \dfrac{1}{\rho v^{2}}, \qquad K = \rho v^{2} \]

where \(f\) is the fixed crystal-driving frequency, \(\overline{\Delta d}\) the mean spacing between successive current maxima, and \(\rho\) the liquid's density.

RF Oscillator Piezoelectric Crystal Liquid Column Micrometer Reflector Current Meter Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A small liquid-filled measuring cell with a piezoelectric quartz crystal fixed at its base (driven by a radio-frequency oscillator) and a movable, micrometer-controlled reflector plate at the top, with a current meter reading the oscillator's anode current.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Driving frequency f 1.0 – 4.0 MHz Liquid selection (density) ρ 780 – 1300 kg/m³ Number of maxima to average over N 5 – 30 —

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Mean spacing between successive maxima Δd mm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Ultrasonic Interferometer panel; select a liquid and set the driving frequency f.
  2. Turn the (virtual) micrometer steadily and note the reflector positions at which the current readout passes through successive maxima.
  3. Record the total distance moved over N consecutive maxima (choosing N as large as practical) and divide by N to get the mean spacing Δd, as shown on the readout.
  4. Repeat for a different driving frequency f and, separately, for a different liquid; tabulate Δd, λ = 2Δd, and v = fλ for each case.
  5. Using the known liquid density ρ, calculate the adiabatic compressibility β and bulk modulus K for each liquid studied.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Wavelength and velocity of ultrasonic waves for different liquids/frequencies

S. No. Liquid f (MHz) N (maxima averaged) Δd (mm) λ = 2Δd (mm) v = fλ (m/s) 1 2 3 4 5

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Total distance moved, D (y-axis) against Number of maxima counted, N (x-axis).

Expected trend: a straight line through the origin, of slope Δd = λ/2, confirming a constant spacing between successive maxima.

Total distance D vs N, expected to be a straight line through the origin; slope = Δd

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Total Distance vs Number of Maxima Number of maxima counted, N Total distance moved, D (mm) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. From the slope of the D vs N graph, obtain the mean maxima spacing Δd = λ/2, hence λ = 2Δd.
  2. Compute v = fλ for each liquid/frequency combination studied.
  3. Using the known density ρ of each liquid, compute the adiabatic compressibility β = 1/(ρv²) and bulk modulus K = ρv².

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Velocity of sound and elastic constants of the liquid(s) studied

Liquid ρ (kg/m³) v (m/s) β (m²/N) K (N/m²) 1 2

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: Averaging the maxima spacing over as many resonances as practical (large N) directly reduces the fractional error in Δd, and hence in v, β and K, since micrometer positioning error is essentially fixed per reading but is divided by N when averaged this way.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Turn the micrometer slowly and always in the same direction while approaching and passing through a current maximum, to avoid backlash error in the screw.
  • Average the maxima spacing over as many resonances as conveniently possible rather than relying on just two consecutive ones, to reduce the fractional error.
  • Keep the liquid free of air bubbles and at a steady temperature, since both dissolved gas and temperature noticeably affect the velocity of sound in a liquid.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. The distance between two successive current maxima in an ultrasonic interferometer corresponds to a reflector movement of:
  • A) One full wavelength, λ
  • B) Half a wavelength, λ/2
  • C) A quarter wavelength, λ/4
  • D) Twice the wavelength, 2λ

Answer: B — Consecutive resonance (standing-wave) conditions occur each time the reflector moves through λ/2, since this brings the reflected wave back into phase with the source wave once more.

2. If the driving frequency f is doubled (all else fixed), the measured wavelength λ in the same liquid:
  • A) Doubles
  • B) Halves
  • C) Stays the same, since v is a material property of the liquid
  • D) Quadruples

Answer: B — Since v = fλ and v is fixed by the liquid (at a given temperature), doubling f must halve λ to keep the product v = fλ unchanged.

3. The adiabatic bulk modulus K of the liquid is calculated from the measured velocity v and density ρ as:
  • A) K = v/ρ
  • B) K = ρv
  • C) K = ρv²
  • D) K = v²/ρ

Answer: C — K = ρv² directly, following from the general relation between the speed of a longitudinal wave in a medium and its elastic modulus and density.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Explain how a standing wave in the liquid column produces a sharp maximum in the crystal's drive current.
  2. Why is it better to measure the total distance over many maxima (large N) and divide, rather than reading a single λ/2 spacing?
  3. Derive the relations for adiabatic compressibility and bulk modulus from the measured velocity and known density.
  4. Why does dissolved air or a temperature change affect the measured velocity of sound in a liquid?
  5. What advantage does the ultrasonic interferometer have over other methods for finding the elastic constants of a liquid?
  6. How would the observed velocity differ between a liquid and, say, air, and what does this reveal about the relative compressibility of liquids versus gases?

docs.title: 6. Uniform Bending
docs.text: ← Back to Course Portal · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: 6. Uniform Bending — Young's Modulus by the Elevation Method
docs.text:
Module

Elasticity of Solids · Module 2

Try it live

Explore this on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To determine the Young's modulus of the material of a beam by the method of uniform bending, using symmetric end-loading and measuring the elevation of the midpoint.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text:
In uniform bending, a beam is supported symmetrically on two knife edges and loaded with equal weights hung symmetrically beyond each knife edge, at equal distances from them. Unlike the cantilever case, the bending moment between the two knife edges is now constant along the entire span — hence the name 'uniform' bending — because the two equal external couples applied beyond each support balance each other identically at every section between the supports.

A constant bending moment means the beam bends into a circular arc of constant curvature between the two knife edges. As a result, the midpoint of the beam rises (or falls, depending on the sense of loading) relative to the two supported points — an elevation that can be measured very precisely using a pin at the centre observed through a travelling microscope, exactly as in the cantilever method.

Because the curvature (and hence the elevation) depends on the elastic properties of the beam in the same way as in non-uniform bending — through the same flexural rigidity Ybd³/12 — this method provides an independent way of extracting Young's modulus, and is often preferred because a uniformly curved beam is easier to analyse exactly and is less sensitive to exactly where along the overhang the load hangers are positioned.

The elevation depends linearly on the load, and on the square of the knife-edge separation, giving two independent straight-line checks (elevation vs load, and vs the square of the separation) of the underlying theory.

docs.title: Governing Formulas
docs.text:
Elevation of the midpoint:

\[ e = \dfrac{3 M g\, a\, l^{2}}{2\, b\, d^{3}\, Y} \]

Young's modulus:

\[ Y = \dfrac{3 M g\, a\, l^{2}}{2\, b\, d^{3}\, e} \]

where \(M\) is each of the two equal end loads, \(a\) the distance from each knife edge to its load point (the overhang), \(l\) the separation between the two knife edges, and \(b\), \(d\) the breadth and thickness of the beam.

Two Knife Edges Beam Symmetric End Loads Travelling Microscope Labelled apparatus layout — original schematic prepared for this guide (not a photograph). A beam resting horizontally on two knife edges with two loop-and-hanger loading points beyond each knife edge at equal overhangs, and a pin at the exact midpoint observed through a travelling microscope to measure elevation.

docs.title: Simulator Controls
docs.text:
Control Symbol Range Unit Each end load M 50 – 500 g Overhang (knife edge to load) a 5 – 20 cm Knife-edge separation l 20 – 60 cm Beam breadth b 1.0 – 3.0 cm Beam thickness d 0.20 – 0.80 cm

Readouts:

Quantity Symbol Unit Elevation of midpoint e mm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Launch the Uniform Bending panel; set b, d, a and l to their default values.
  2. Vary the symmetric end load M in equal steps and record the elevation e of the midpoint shown on the readout for each step.
  3. Plot e against M and verify a straight line through the origin.
  4. Reset M to a middle value; vary the knife-edge separation l and tabulate e for each l; verify that e ∝ l² by plotting e against l².
  5. Calculate Young's modulus from the slope of the e vs M graph, using the known a, l, b, d.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text:
Table: Elevation of the midpoint with increasing symmetric end load

S. No. M (g) a (cm) l (cm) Elevation e (mm) 1 2 3 4 5 6

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Plot Elevation, e (y-axis) against Each end load, M (x-axis).

Expected trend: a straight line through the origin (e ∝ M).

e vs M, expected to be a straight line through the origin; slope = 3gal²/(2bd³Y)

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your camera/DSO/graph trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Elevation vs End Load Each end load, M (g) Elevation, e (mm) 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table

docs.title: Calculation
docs.text:
1. Compute the slope of the e vs M graph: slope = 3gal²/(2bd³Y).
  2. Rearrange to find Y = 3gal²/(2bd³ × slope), using the fixed a, l, b, d for that run.
  3. Cross-check using the l-sweep data: from the slope of e vs l², estimate Y independently.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Table: Young's modulus of the beam material by uniform bending

Trial a (cm) l (cm) Slope (mm/g) Y (dyne/cm²) 1 2 3

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text: As in the cantilever method, the beam thickness d enters cubed, so its measurement uncertainty (via a screw gauge) usually dominates the total error.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Load the two hangers with exactly equal weights at exactly equal overhangs, to preserve the symmetric, constant-bending-moment condition the formula assumes.
  • Take microscope readings only after oscillations have died out and for both loading and unloading, averaging the two.
  • Ensure the beam rests freely on the knife edges (not clamped), since the derivation assumes simply-supported, not built-in, end conditions.

docs.title: Self-Check Quiz
docs.text:
1. The bending moment between the two knife edges in uniform bending is:
  • A) Zero
  • B) Constant along the whole span
  • C) Varying linearly along the span
  • D) Varying as the square of the distance

Answer: B — This constancy is exactly why the method is called 'uniform' bending, and is why the beam bends into a circular arc between the supports.

2. If the knife-edge separation l is doubled (all else fixed), the elevation e:
  • A) Doubles
  • B) Increases fourfold
  • C) Halves
  • D) Stays the same

Answer: B — e ∝ l², so doubling l increases e by a factor of 4.

3. Compared to non-uniform (cantilever) bending, uniform bending measures deflection:
  • A) At the clamped end
  • B) At the free end of a single cantilever
  • C) At the midpoint between two symmetrically loaded supports
  • D) At the knife edges themselves

Answer: C — The midpoint elevation, relative to the (also slightly depressed) knife-edge supports, is what is measured in uniform bending.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why is the bending moment constant between the knife edges in this experiment, whereas it varies along a cantilever?
  2. What experimental conditions are essential to ensure the 'uniform' bending assumption actually holds?
  3. Why does the elevation depend on the square of the knife-edge separation, rather than its cube as in the cantilever case?
  4. How would you check, using your own data, whether the beam is within its elastic limit throughout the range of loads used?
  5. Compare the sensitivity of Y to the thickness d in this method versus the cantilever method.
  6. Why is symmetric loading (equal M, equal a on both sides) essential to the validity of the formula used?
